text:
Information Retrieval
• Information Retrieval (IR) is about finding 
material (usually documents) of an unstructured
nature (usually text) that satisfies an information 
need from within large digital collections.
– These days we frequently think of web search first, 
but there are many other cases:
• E-mail search
• Searching your laptop
• Corporate knowledge bases
• Legal information retrieval
3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

text:
IR Origins
•
The “information overload” 
problem is much older than you 
may think, much before the WEB
•
Origins in period immediately 
after World War II
–
Tremendous scientific progress 
during the war
–
Rapid growth in the number of 
scientific publications available
•
The “Memex Machine”  
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Memex
–
Conceived by Vannevar Bush, 
President Roosevelt's science advisor
–
Foreshadows the development of 
hypertext (the Web) and information 
retrieval systems
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

text:
The Memex Machine
Consider a future device for individual use, which 
is a sort of mechanized private file and library. It 
needs a name, and, to coin one at random, 
“memex” will do. A memex is a device in which 
an individual stores all his books, records, and 
communications, and which is mechanized so 
that it may be consulted with exceeding speed 
and flexibility. It is an enlarged intimate 
supplement to his memory. 
It consists of a desk, and while it can presumably 
be operated from a distance, it is primarily the 
piece of furniture at which he works. On the top 
are slanting translucent screens, on which 
material can be projected for convenient reading. 
There is a keyboard and sets of buttons and 
levers. Otherwise, it looks like an ordinary desk.
(Vannevar Bush; As We May Think; 
Atlantic Monthly; July 1945)
Only to browse, not to search.
[NOTE] Only to browse, not to search.

Search

– scale of tens of millions of content creators.
• Continuous optimization of IR effectiveness (and

quality levels
– thus, becoming the preferred source of information finding

text: Web Search Engines (WSEs)
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

text:
WSE definition
• A Web Search Engine is a complex software system 
designed to search for information on the World Wide Web 
– Users enter keywords or phrases (queries), and the WSE returns a 
ranked list of relevant websites
• A Web Search Engine mainly consists of:
– Crawler (Spider/Bot): Collects web pages
– Indexing System: Organizes, stores and indexes pages for fast 
retrieval
– Query processor: Determines the relevance of pages to a given 
query, and rank the relevant pages
WSE vs IR: 
Downloads pages and creates a database?
Permeates faster retrival

[NOTE] Downloads pages and creates a database?
[NOTE] Permeates faster retrival

text:
Generative AI and IR
• ChatGPT / Gemini [since 2022] are conversational  
chatbots based on Generative AI
– Large Language Model (LLM) 
– LLMs trained (through self-supervised learning) on huge text 
corpora to model the probability distribution of language
– chatbots generate responses, given a long enough input text (a 
prompt, expressed in natural language), token-by-token (terms or 
portions of terms) based on learned statistical patterns
– In principle, at inference time, they do not need to query an external 
database of documents … but they do when specific triggers occur
• For example, if a prompt requires real-time information, the chatbot
autonomously write a query for a search engine (e.g., for Google or 
Bing), retrieve the top results, and use them to synthesize the answer
• This tries to avoid hallucinations, but in this last case a chatbot
works like a  RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
all the possible next words given all the previous ones.
[NOTE] all the possible next words given all the previous ones.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

text:
IR systems vs. Generative AI
• Search Engines vs. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
– IR systems (search engines) can now operate in a pipeline that in 
some cases includes Generative-AI
– In such architectures, retrieval provides relevant documents (or 
passages/chunks of these documents)
– In some cases, the IR system performs content synthesis and 
generation using LLM
• For example, AI overview by Google
– Issues:
• AI Overviews can hallucinate when retrieval returns irrelevant 
documents, especially for obscure or complex queries.
• Generative AI is expensive, and thus for efficiency reasons, it is not 
used for Navigational and Simple Queries
• In summary: if the query is too simple, too fresh, or also too risky, the 
classic "10 blue links" take over.
Might be due to IR failure too.
[NOTE] Might be due to IR failure too.
[NOTE] If something has happened a minute ago, chances are it's not indexd for LLM yet.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture0-intro.pdf

information
– Relevance (Effectiveness)

– attract more users
– increase the ads (advertising) revenue
– reduce the operational costs
•

– attract more users to their site
– pay little

sorted list of links + sponsored 
ads

Advertisers bid on keywords 
(e.g., “best running shoes”).
•
Revenue = pay-per-click (PPC)
•
More clicks, more money

text:
AI impact on SE Business Models
1.
Advertising Evolution (Ads Inside the AI-generated Answer)
2.
Consumer Subscriptions (Freemium Search)
–
Massive computing power required by Generative AI: expensive wrt traditional 
searches
3.
Data Licensing (significant new cost for SE companies)
–
Old model, SEs scraped publishers' websites for free, and in exchange, sent them 
free user traffic
–
AI overview over search results often gives the user the answer without sending 
traffic back to the original website
–
Publishers ask for licensing deals (e.g., Reddit, News Corp) just to legally train their 
AI models and display their content in real-time as AI overview
4.
Zero-click searches are reshaping SEO (SE Optimization) strategies
–
up to 60-70% of local searches (services near to me) are now becoming "zero-
click”, as they are satified by AI-generated summary
–
small businesses can no longer just fight to be "Rank #1." They have to fight to be 
the source the AI quotes. This new strategy is called GEO (Generative Engine 
Optimization) or AEO (Answer Engine Optimization). Generative AI doesn't use the whole document
[NOTE] Generative AI doesn't use the whole document

text:
Let’s start with a simple Boolean
retrieval model 
(AND/OR/NOT operators)
• Which plays of Shakespeare contain the following words?
         Brutus AND Caesar  but NOT Calpurnia
• One could grep all of Shakespeare’s plays for Brutus and 
Caesar, then strip out lines containing Calpurnia?
• Why is that not the answer?
– Slow (for large corpora)
– NOT Calpurnia is non-trivial
– Other operations (e.g., find the word Romans near countrymen) 
not feasible
– Ranked retrieval (best documents to return)
• Later lectures
8

text:
Where do we place skips?
• Tradeoff:
– More skips → shorter skip spans  more likely to 
skip.  But lots of comparisons to skip pointers.
– Fewer skips → few pointer comparison, but then 
long skip spans  few successful skips.
Sec. 2.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

text:
Placing skips
• Simple heuristic: for postings of length L, use 𝐿 evenly-
spaced skip pointers     [Moffat and Zobel 1996]
– This ignores the distribution of query terms.
– Easy if the index is relatively static; harder if  L keeps changing 
because of updates.
• Researchers showed good performance gains from 
skipping
– wrt a system architecture designed to optimize for the large 
memory spaces and multiple CPU cores [Strohman and Croft (2007)] 
• Skip lists can be combined with compression of 
portions/blocks of the lists, thus avoiding decompressing 
skipped sub-lists/blocks
Sec. 2.3

def union(p1, p2):
    answer = []
    while p1 != [] and p2 != []:
        if p1[0] == p2[0] :
            answer.append(p1[0])
            p1 = p1[1:]
            p2 = p2[1:]
        elif p1[0] < p2[0]:
            answer.append(p1[0])
            p1 = p1[1:]
        else:
            answer.append(p2[0])
            p2 = p2[1:]
    if p1 == [] and p2 != []:
        answer.extend(p2)
    if p2 == [] and p1 != []:
        answer.extend(p1)
    return answer

Boolean Model

source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

text:
Boolean queries: Exact match
• The Boolean retrieval model is being able to ask a 
query that is a Boolean expression:
– Boolean Queries are queries using AND, OR and NOT 
to join query terms
• View each document as a set of words
• Are precise: document matches condition or not.
– Perhaps the simplest model to build an IR system
• Primary commercial retrieval tool for 3 decades. 
• Many search systems you still use are Boolean:
– Email, library catalog, etc.
50
Sec. 1.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

text:
Why Boolean Retrieval Works
• Boolean operators approximate natural language
– Find documents about a good party that is not over
• AND can discover relationships between concepts
– good party
• OR can discover alternate terminology
– excellent party, wild party, etc.
• NOT can exclude unintended meanings
– Republican party
(good OR excellent OR wild) AND Party AND 
NOT (Republican AND party)
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture1-boolean model.pdf

text:
Why Boolean Retrieval Fails
• Natural language is way more complex
• AND “discovers” nonexistent relationships
– Terms in different sentences, paragraphs, …
• Guessing terminology for OR is hard
– good, nice, excellent, outstanding, awesome, …
• Guessing terms to exclude is even harder!
– Republican party, party to a lawsuit, …

text:
Boolean IR Model 
+ new proximity and partial match operators
WestLaw   http://www.westlaw.com/
(Legal Information Retrieval)
• Largest “commercial” legal search service (started 1975; 
ranking added 1992; new federated search added 2010)
• Tens of terabytes of data; ~700,000 users
• Majority of users still use Boolean queries, although other 
methods are available
• Example query:
– What is the statute of limitations in cases involving the federal tort 
claims act?
– LIMIT! /3 STATUTE ACTION /S FEDERAL /2 TORT /3 CLAIM
• /s, /p, and /k ask for matches in the same sentence, same 
paragraph or within k words respectively, ! gives a trailing 
wildcard query 
• SPACE is disjunction, not conjunction!

text:
• Web partitioning
– hash-based partitioning: based on the MD5 hashes of 
either canonical URLs or host names
– site-based partitioning: all pages of a website are assigned 
to the same crawler node. 
Motivation: with URL/hash-based partitioning URLs 
managed by the same web host may be hashed to distinct 
partitions, thus leading to politeness issues as crawling 
decisions (made by individual nodes) are not coordinated
• Fault tolerance
– when a crawling node dies, its URLs are redistributed over 
the remaining crawler nodes
Web Partitioning and Fault Tolerance

text:
URL frontier: two main considerations
▪Politeness: do not hit a web server too frequently
▪Freshness: crawl some pages more often than others
▪E.g., pages (such as News sites) whose content changes 
often

two separate queues for prioritizing the download of

text:
• How do general-purpose SEs solve this the freshness issue?
• They use two or more refreshing queues, with different turnout 
times
• A queue for web sites to be refreshed several time a day
• A queue for web sites to be refreshed daily
• A queue for web sites to be refreshed weekly, for popular or 
relevant one (e.g., relevant wrt PageRank)
• A larger queue for pages to be refreshed every month or 
every few months

text:
Normalization: other languages
▪Accents: e.g., French résumé vs. resume.
▪Umlauts: e.g., German: Tuebingen vs. Tübingen
▪Should be equivalent
▪Most important criterion:
▪How are your users like to write their queries for these 
words?
▪Even in languages that standardly have accents, users 
often may not type them
▪Often best to normalize to a de-accented term
▪Tuebingen, Tübingen, Tubingen  → Tubingen
▪Crucial: Need to “normalize” indexed text as well as 
query terms identically

text:
Stop words
▪With a stop list, you exclude from the dictionary 
entirely the commonest words. Intuition:
▪They have little semantic content: the, a, and, to, be
▪There are a lot of them: ~30% of postings for top 30 words
▪But the trend is away from doing this:
▪Good compression techniques (IIR 5) means the space for including 
stop words in a system is very small
▪Good query optimization techniques (IIR 7) mean you pay little at 
query time for including stop words.
▪You need them for:
▪Phrase queries: “King of Denmark”
▪Various song titles, etc.: “Let it be”, “To be or not to be”
▪Relational queries: “flights to Venice”
Sec. 2.2.2

text:
Porter’s algorithm
▪Commonest algorithm for stemming English
▪The Porter stemming algorithm (1980) reduces English 
words to their morphological stems through a sequence of 
rule-based reductions.
▪Phases of reductions
▪phases applied sequentially
▪each phase consists of a set of commands
▪Sample convention: Of the rules in a compound command 
in a given phase, select the one that applies to the longest 
matching suffix to be removed

the rule a suffix to replace, rather than a part of the steam of the 
word ?

text:
How to input text into a DNN for IR
▪LLMs such as ChatGPT and GPT-4 also used for Neural-IR
▪They tokenizes input and output text using a technique 
called byte pair encoding (BPE)
▪LLMs learn to understand statistical relationships between 
these tokens, associated with vector embeddings
1. Initial Vocabulary: Initially, each character in the text is treated as a token
2. Tokenization: The algorithm iteratively merges the most frequent pair of 
consecutive tokens (characters or subwords) to form a new token. This process 
is repeated until a predefined vocabulary size is reached.
3. Building Vocabulary: As tokens are merged, the vocabulary grows, and the

text:
How to input text into a deep neural
network for IR
▪WordPiece Tokenizer for BERT 
1. Basic Tokenization: BERT first tokenize the input text into words using a 
basic tokenizer. This tokenizer typically splits the text on spaces and 
punctuation
2. WordPiece Tokenization: After basic tokenization, BERT applies WordPiece
tokenization. This method further breaks down words into smaller 
subword units called WordPieces. 
3. Vocabulary Building: The initial vocabulary consists of individual 
characters and some common subword units. BERT updates this 
vocabulary by learning which subword units are most useful for 
representing the language. Like BPE, also WordPiece exploits the 
frequency by merging the initial vocabulary.

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪
Generalization of binary trees
▪
Nodes can have more than two children
▪
Because a range of child nodes is permitted, B-trees do not need re-balancing 
as frequently as other trees
▪
In a B-tree of order m, each nonleaf node has up to m children
▪
If a node has n <= m children, it contains n−1 keys 
▪
The keys act as separation values dividing its subtrees

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪
Binary search trees are not good for spatial locality 
▪
a given node of a binary tree probably occupies only a fraction of any 
memory block (on disks or caches)
▪
B-trees are a way to get better locality by putting multiple elements into each 
tree node/memory block 
▪
B-trees were originally invented for storing data structures on disks, 
where locality is even more crucial than with main memory
▪
Nowadays B-trees may also be useful for in-memory data structures 
▪
Main memory is nowadays very slow relative to the processor, and thus 
effective cache exploitation is crucial for efficient algorithms

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪A B-tree of order m is a search tree in which each nonleaf node has 
up to m children
▪In-memory B-tree
▪We can choose m so that the pointers to the m children plus 
the m−1 elements fill out a cache line at the highest level of the memory 
hierarchy (where we can expect to get cache hits)
▪Each leaf node stores some number of elements: the maximum number is 
typically less than or equal to m (but can also be a multiple of m)

text:
Trees
▪Simplest: binary tree
▪More usual: B-trees
▪Trees, unlike hashtables, require a standard ordering of 
characters and hence strings … but we typically have one
▪Lexicographic order
▪Pros:
▪Solves the prefix problem (terms starting with hyp)
▪Cons:
▪Slower: O(log M)  [and this requires balanced tree]
▪Rebalancing binary trees is expensive
▪But B-trees mitigate the rebalancing problem

▪With 3-grams, considering the leading and trailing $

▪we have a size per term equal to   n * 3 chars

text:
Spell correction
▪Two principal uses
▪Correcting document(s) being indexed
▪Correcting user queries to retrieve “right” answers
▪Two main flavors:
▪Isolated word
▪Check each word on its own for misspelling
▪Will not catch typos resulting in correctly spelled words
 
 
e.g., from → form
▪Context-sensitive
▪Look at surrounding words, 
 
 
e.g., I flew form Heathrow to Narita.

before running the queries

text:
Document correction
▪Especially needed for OCR’ed documents
▪Correction algorithms are tuned for specific cases: rn/m
▪Can use domain-specific knowledge
▪E.g., OCR can confuse O and D more often than it would confuse O 
and I (adjacent on the QWERTY keyboard, so more likely 
interchanged in typing).
▪But also: web pages and even printed material have 
typos
▪Goal: index dictionary containing fewer misspellings
Sec. 3.3

text:
Query mis-spellings
▪Our principal focus here
▪e.g., the query Alanis Morisett    (correct query: Alanis 
Morissette)
▪We can either
▪Directly retrieve documents indexed by the most likely 
correct spelling
  
OR
▪Return several suggested alternative queries with the 
correct spelling
▪Did you mean … ?

text:
Isolated word correction
▪Fundamental premise – there is a (correct) lexicon 
from which the correct spellings come
▪Two basic choices for this
▪A standard lexicon such as
▪Webster’s English Dictionary
▪An “industry-specific” lexicon – hand-maintained
▪Extreme choice: the lexicon of the indexed corpus
▪e.g., all words on the web
▪All names, acronyms etc.
▪Including the mis-spellings  
Sec. 3.3.2
31
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval.pdf

text:
Isolated word correction
▪PROBLEM STATEMENT
▪Given a lexicon and a character sequence Q, return the 
words in the lexicon closest to Q
▪What’s “closest”?
▪We’ll study several alternatives
▪Edit distance (Levenshtein distance)
▪Weighted edit distance
▪n-gram overlap (mainly to reduce the search space) 
▪Principles
▪Among alternative correct spellings for a mis-spelled 
query, choose the “nearest” one
▪If two correctly spelled queries are tied (or nearly tied), 
select the most common (in collection or query log)
Sec. 3.3.2
32
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval.pdf

text:
Edit distance
▪Edit distance definition
▪Given two strings S1 and S2, the minimum number of 
operations to convert one to the other
▪Operations are typically character-level
▪Insert, Delete, Replace, (Transposition)
▪E.g., the edit distance from dof to dog is 1 (replace)
▪From cat to act is 2  (Just 1 with transpose)
▪from cat to dog is 3 
▪Generally found by a dynamic programming algorithm.
▪The  time to compute the edit distance between two strings is 
O(|S1| × | S2|), where |Si| denotes the length of a string
Sec. 3.3.3
33
From -transpose-> Form
If they don't share any characters, then distance is num char(max(s1, s2)).
so for cat and dog, you will need 3*3 = 9 operations to realize that the distance is 3.
[NOTE] From -transpose-> Form
[NOTE] If they don't share any characters, then distance is num char(max(s1, s2)).
[NOTE] so for cat and dog, you will need 3*3 = 9 operations to realize that the distance is 3.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval.pdf

text:
Defining edit distance
▪For two strings
▪X of length n
▪Y of length m 
   define D(i,j)
▪the edit distance between X[1..i] and Y[1..j] 
▪i.e., between the first i characters of X and the first j 
characters of Y 
▪The edit distance between X and Y is thus D(n,m)

text:
Dynamic Programming for Min Edit Distance
▪Dynamic programming
▪A general method of breaking down a larger problem into sub-problems
▪If you work through the sub-problems in the right order, building each answer 
based on the previous one, you eventually solve the larger problem 
▪Min Edit Distance solved with Dynamic programming 
▪A tabular computation of D(n,m) 
▪Bottom-up: solving problems by combining solutions to subproblems 
▪Computation
▪Compute D(i,j) for small i,j 
▪Compute larger D(i,j) based on previously computed smaller values
▪Eventually, compute D(i,j) forall i (0<i<n)  and j (0<j<m), and return D(n,m)

▪This may be formulated as a probability model
▪Requires weight matrix as input

text:
Distance to all dictionary terms? Unfeasible!
How can we reduce the search space?
▪Given a (mis-spelled) word – we need to compute its 
edit distance to every dictionary term to implement the 
distance-based range query
▪Expensive and slow
▪How do we cut the set of candidate dictionary terms?
▪Restrict the search to dictionary terms beginning with the 
same letter as the query string (naïve solution)
▪Generate everything up to a small edit distance (k=1,2,..) and 
then intersect these candidates with terms in the index lexicon
▪Another possibility is to use n-gram overlap
▪The same used for wildcard queries
▪Correct and misspelled terms likely share some/many n-grams

text:
n-gram overlap
▪Enumerate all the n-grams in the misspelled query 
string (as done for the lexicon at index construction)
▪Use the n-gram index (the same introduced for wild-
card search) to retrieve all lexicon terms matching any 
of the query’s n-grams
▪Set of candidate terms, from which we need to filter out 
terms that are not the correct ones with high probability
▪Thresholding on the number of matching n-grams
▪We filter out terms, where the number of matching n-
grams is smaller than the threshold
▪Variants – weight by keyboard layout, etc.

▪Suppose the text is november

▪The Set-Union is 9 trigrams, while the Set-Intersection is 3 
trigrams (3 of 9)
▪If the overlap is “high” enough
▪december may be the mispelled term of november

text:
Can we use Jaccard coefficient?
▪A commonly-used measure of set overlap
▪Let X and Y be two sets; then the Jaccard Coefficient is
▪Equals 1 when X and Y have the same elements, and 0 
when they are disjoint (similarity measure)
▪X and Y don’t have to be of the same size
▪Always assigns a number between 0 and 1
▪Exploit a threshold to decide if you have a possible “match”
▪e.g., if J ⩾ 0.8, declare a match 
▪In the previous example J = 3/9 = 0.33

and minimum dissimilarities.

Context-sensitive correction
▪Need surrounding context to catch this.
▪First idea: retrieve dictionary terms close (in 
weighted edit distance) to each query term
▪Using the previous methods
▪Now try all possible resulting phrase queries for “flew form 
heathrow”, with one word “fixed” at a time
▪flew from heathrow 
▪fled form heathrow
▪flea form heathrow
▪Hit-based spelling correction: Suggest the 
alternative that has lots of hits
▪expensive if many corrections of individual terms

Another approach
▪As we expand the alternatives for flew and form
▪we retain only the most frequent combinations not in the 
collection, but in the query logs (which contain previous 
queries by users) 
▪Break phrase query into a conjunction of biwords 
(Lecture 2).
▪Look for biwords that need only one term corrected, given 
flew from:
▪the biword fled form is likely to be rare compared to the biword 
flew from 
▪Then we only attempt to extend the list of the top biwords

text:
Hashtables
▪Each vocabulary term is hashed to an integer
▪(We assume you’ve seen hashtables before)
▪Pros:
▪Lookup is faster than for a tree: O(1)
▪Cons:
▪No easy way to find minor variants:
▪judgment/judgement   [two different correct spellings)
▪No prefix search with wildcard  
[tolerant  retrieval]
▪If vocabulary keeps growing, need to occasionally do the 
expensive operation of rehashing everything

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪
Generalization of binary trees
▪
Nodes can have more than two children
▪
Because a range of child nodes is permitted, B-trees do not need re-balancing 
as frequently as other trees
▪
In a B-tree of order m, each nonleaf node has up to m children
▪
If a node has n <= m children, it contains n−1 keys 
▪
The keys act as separation values dividing its subtrees

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪
Binary search trees are not good for spatial locality 
▪
a given node of a binary tree probably occupies only a fraction of any 
memory block (on disks or caches)
▪
B-trees are a way to get better locality by putting multiple elements into each 
tree node/memory block 
▪
B-trees were originally invented for storing data structures on disks, 
where locality is even more crucial than with main memory
▪
Nowadays B-trees may also be useful for in-memory data structures 
▪
Main memory is nowadays very slow relative to the processor, and thus 
effective cache exploitation is crucial for efficient algorithms

text:
Tree: B-tree
▪A B-tree of order m is a search tree in which each nonleaf node has 
up to m children
▪In-memory B-tree
▪We can choose m so that the pointers to the m children plus 
the m−1 elements fill out a cache line at the highest level of the memory 
hierarchy (where we can expect to get cache hits)
▪Each leaf node stores some number of elements: the maximum number is 
typically less than or equal to m (but can also be a multiple of m)
Sec. 3.1

text:
Trees
▪Simplest: binary tree
▪More usual: B-trees
▪Trees, unlike hashtables, require a standard ordering of 
characters and hence strings … but we typically have one
▪Lexicographic order
▪Pros:
▪Solves the prefix problem (terms starting with hyp)
▪Cons:
▪Slower: O(log M)  [and this requires balanced tree]
▪Rebalancing binary trees is expensive
▪But B-trees mitigate the rebalancing problem

text:
Permuterm vs 2-grams vs 3-grams
▪Permuterm is not space efficient
▪hello$, ello$h, llo$he, lo$hel, o$hell, $hello
▪n is the average length of a term
▪using $ as suffix, we add further n rotations to vocabulary
▪size for a term of n chars: 
(n+1) * (n+1) chars
▪With 2-grams, considering the leading and trailing $
▪$h, he, el, ll, lo, o$
▪we have a size per term equal to   (n+1) * 2 chars
▪With 3-grams, considering the leading and trailing $
▪$he, hel, ell, llo, lo$
▪we have a size per term equal to   n * 3 chars

text:
SPELLING CORRECTION
27
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Spell correction
▪Two principal uses
▪Correcting document(s) being indexed
▪Correcting user queries to retrieve “right” answers
▪Two main flavors:
▪Isolated word
▪Check each word on its own for misspelling
▪Will not catch typos resulting in correctly spelled words
 
 
e.g., from → form
▪Context-sensitive
▪Look at surrounding words, 
 
 
e.g., I flew form Heathrow to Narita.
Sec. 3.3
28
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Document correction
▪Especially needed for OCR’ed documents
▪Correction algorithms are tuned for specific cases: rn/m
▪Can use domain-specific knowledge
▪E.g., OCR can confuse O and D more often than it would confuse O 
and I (adjacent on the QWERTY keyboard, so more likely 
interchanged in typing).
▪But also: web pages and even printed material have 
typos
▪Goal: index dictionary containing fewer misspellings
Sec. 3.3
29
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Query mis-spellings
▪Our principal focus here
▪e.g., the query Alanis Morisett    (correct query: Alanis 
Morissette)
▪We can either
▪Directly retrieve documents indexed by the most likely 
correct spelling
  
OR
▪Return several suggested alternative queries with the 
correct spelling
▪Did you mean … ?
Sec. 3.3
30
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Isolated word correction
▪Fundamental premise – there is a (correct) lexicon 
from which the correct spellings come
▪Two basic choices for this
▪A standard lexicon such as
▪Webster’s English Dictionary
▪An “industry-specific” lexicon – hand-maintained
▪Extreme choice: the lexicon of the indexed corpus
▪e.g., all words on the web
▪All names, acronyms etc.
▪Including the mis-spellings  
Sec. 3.3.2
31
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Isolated word correction
▪PROBLEM STATEMENT
▪Given a lexicon and a character sequence Q, return the 
words in the lexicon closest to Q
▪What’s “closest”?
▪We’ll study several alternatives
▪Edit distance (Levenshtein distance)
▪Weighted edit distance
▪n-gram overlap (mainly to reduce the search space) 
▪Principles
▪Among alternative correct spellings for a mis-spelled 
query, choose the “nearest” one
▪If two correctly spelled queries are tied (or nearly tied), 
select the most common (in collection or query log)
Sec. 3.3.2
32
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Edit distance
▪Edit distance definition
▪Given two strings S1 and S2, the minimum number of 
operations to convert one to the other
▪Operations are typically character-level
▪Insert, Delete, Replace, (Transposition)
▪E.g., the edit distance from dof to dog is 1 (replace)
▪From cat to act is 2  (Just 1 with transpose)
▪from cat to dog is 3 
▪Generally found by a dynamic programming algorithm.
▪The  time to compute the edit distance between two strings is 
O(|S1| × | S2|), where |Si| denotes the length of a string
Sec. 3.3.3
33
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Defining edit distance
▪For two strings
▪X of length n
▪Y of length m 
   define D(i,j)
▪the edit distance between prefixes: X[1..i] and Y[1..j] 
▪i.e., between the first i characters of X and the first j 
characters of Y 
▪the edit distance between X and Y is thus D(n,m) 
Sec. 3.3.3
34
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Dynamic Programming for Min Edit Distance
▪Dynamic programming
▪A general method of breaking down a larger problem into sub-problems
▪If you work through the sub-problems in the right order, building each answer 
based on the previous one, you eventually solve the larger problem 
▪Min Edit Distance solved with Dynamic programming 
▪A tabular computation of D(n,m) 
▪Bottom-up: solving problems by combining solutions to subproblems 
▪Computation
▪Compute D(i,j) for small i,j 
▪Compute larger D(i,j) based on previously computed smaller values
▪Eventually, compute D(i,j) forall i (0<i<n)  and j (0<j<m), and return D(n,m) 
Sec. 3.3.3
35
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Dynamic Programming for Min Edit Distance
Sec. 3.3.3
36
If the last characters of X[1..i] and 
Y[1..j] are different, then the edit 
distance is equal to 
the minimum of the cost of 
deleting / inserting the last 
character of string Y
first i chars of X to "", delete all i of them
"" to first j chars of Y, append j chars to ""
[NOTE] first i chars of X to "", delete all i of them

"" to first j chars of Y, append j chars to ""

text:
Weighted edit distance
▪As above, but the weight of an operation depends on 
the character(s) involved
▪Meant to capture OCR or keyboard errors
Example: m more likely to be mis-typed as n than as q
▪Therefore, replacing m by n is a smaller edit distance than 
by q
▪This may be formulated as a probability model
▪Requires weight matrix as input
▪We can modify the dynamic programming algorithm 
to handle weights
Sec. 3.3.3
37
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
How to use edit distance for spelling 
checking 
▪Given a misspelled word, enumerate all “correct” 
words up to a preset distance (distance-based 
range query)
1. Show terms you found to user as suggestions
2. Alternatively, 
▪We can look up all possible corrections … slow
▪We can run with a single most likely correction (top-
1 result)
▪The alternatives disempower the user, but save a 
round of interaction with the user
Sec. 3.3.4
38
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Distance to all dictionary terms? Unfeasible!
How can we reduce the search space?
▪Given a (mis-spelled) word – we need to compute its 
edit distance to every dictionary term to implement the 
distance-based range query
▪Expensive and slow
▪How do we cut the set of candidate dictionary terms?
▪Restrict the search to dictionary terms beginning with the 
same letter as the query string (naïve solution)
▪Generate everything up to a small edit distance (k=1,2,..) and 
then intersect these candidates with terms in the index lexicon
▪Another possibility is to use n-gram overlap
▪The same used for wildcard queries
▪Correct and misspelled terms likely share some/many n-grams
Sec. 3.3.4
39
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
n-gram overlap
▪Enumerate all the n-grams in the misspelled query 
string (as done for the lexicon at index construction)
▪Use the n-gram index (the same introduced for wild-
card search) to retrieve all lexicon terms matching any 
of the query’s n-grams
▪Set of candidate terms, from which we need to filter out 
terms that are not the correct ones with high probability
▪Thresholding on the number of matching n-grams
▪We filter out terms, where the number of matching n-
grams is smaller than the threshold
▪Variants – weight by keyboard layout, etc.
Sec. 3.3.4
40
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Example with trigrams
▪Suppose the text is november
▪Trigrams are nov, ove, vem, emb, mbe, ber.
▪The query is december
▪Trigrams are dec, ece, cem, emb, mbe, ber.
▪So, 3 trigrams overlap (of 6 in each term)
▪The Set-Union is 9 trigrams, while the Set-Intersection is 3 
trigrams (3 of 9)
▪If the overlap is “high” enough
▪december may be the mispelled term of november   
              ← edit distance = 3 (3 replacement)
▪How can we turn this into a normalized measure of 
overlap?
Sec. 3.3.4
41
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Can we use Jaccard coefficient?
▪A commonly-used measure of set overlap
▪Let X and Y be two sets; then the Jaccard Coefficient is
▪Equals 1 when X and Y have the same elements, and 0 
when they are disjoint (similarity measure)
▪X and Y don’t have to be of the same size
▪Always assigns a number between 0 and 1
▪Exploit a threshold to decide if you have a possible “match”
▪e.g., if J ⩾ 0.8, declare a match 
▪In the previous example J = 3/9 = 0.33
Sec. 3.3.4

Context-sensitive spell correction
▪Text: I flew from Heathrow to Narita.
▪Consider the phrase query “flew form Heathrow”
▪We’d like to respond
  Did you mean “flew from Heathrow”?
because no docs matched the phrase query

source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Context-sensitive correction
▪Need surrounding context to catch this.
▪First idea: retrieve dictionary terms close (in 
weighted edit distance) to each query term
▪Using the previous methods
▪Now try all possible resulting phrase queries for “flew form 
heathrow”, with one word “fixed” at a time
▪flew from heathrow 
▪fled form heathrow
▪flea form heathrow
▪Hit-based spelling correction: Suggest the 
alternative that has lots of hits
▪expensive if many corrections of individual terms
Sec. 3.3.5
46
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Another approach
▪As we expand the alternatives for flew and form
▪we retain only the most frequent combinations not in the 
collection, but in the query logs (which contain previous 
queries by users) 
▪Break phrase query into a conjunction of biwords 
(Lecture 2).
▪Look for biwords that need only one term corrected, given 
flew from:
▪the biword fled form is likely to be rare compared to the biword 
flew from 
▪Then we only attempt to extend the list of the top biwords 
(such as flew from) to possible corrections of Heathrow 
Sec. 3.3.5

text:
Modern LLM-based approaches
▪LLMs can avoid to use a dictionary-based spell checker
▪Instead, the estimates:
▪𝑃(next_word ∣ previous_language_patterns)
▪LLMs has learned from massive text corpora that:
▪“I flew from heathrow” is extremely common
▪“I flew form heathrow” is extremely rare
▪So the model compares probabilities:
▪𝑃(from ∣I flew)  ≫𝑃(form ∣I flew)
▪It chooses the word that makes the sentence more 
statistically likely.
Sec. 3.3.5
49
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture3-tolerant-retrieval2.pdf

text:
Modern LLM-based approaches
▪LLMs like BERT (by Google) use a technique called
Masked Language Modeling (MLM): "Fill-in-the-Blanks"
▪Training:  Millions of sentences, blanked out a random word, 
and forced BERT to guess (probability distribution of words) 
what was missing (based on the context words around it)
▪How to use BERT as a contextual spellchecker:
▪Feed BERT your sentence, but hide the suspicious word: 
▪"I flew [MASK] Heathrow to Venice"
▪BERT reads bidirectionally (looking at the words before
and after the blank)
▪BERT will predict with extremely high mathematical probability
that the [MASK] should be the word «from» (it highest
likelihood)
Sec. 3.3.5
50
This will be much better when there aren't many options to fill the mask.

text:
Hardware basics
▪Many design decisions in information retrieval are 
based on the characteristics of hardware
▪We begin by reviewing hardware basics
Sec. 4.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture4-indexconstruction.pdf

text:
Hardware basics
▪Access to data in memory (DRAM) is much faster than access to 
data on disk (Hard Disk Drive - HDD)
▪Latencies of HDD vs DRAM: DRAM 6/7 orders of magnitude faster than HDD
▪Disk seek/rotation issues: No data is transferred from disk during 
head seeks(rotations), and data must be transferred in large blocks
▪Disk I/O is block-based: Reading and writing of entire blocks (as opposed to 
smaller ones) once r/w head is positioned on the correct sector
▪Block sizes: 8KB to 256 KB.
▪Solid State Drive (SSD) is still block-based, but 100x faster than 
HDD, whereas seeks do not apply
▪Note that also caches works by transferring blocks (from/to DRAM)
▪i.e., blocks = cache lines
▪Caches 2/3 order of magnitude faster than DRAM
Sec. 4.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture4-indexconstruction.pdf

text:
Hardware basics
▪Servers used in IR systems now typically have several 
GBs of main memory
▪Available disk space is however several (2–3) orders of 
magnitude larger.
▪Fault tolerance is very expensive
▪It’s much cheaper to use many regular machines (high 
level of redundancy, as indexes are mainly read-only) 
rather than one big fault-tolerant machine.

To do this, it must simultaneously build and maintain a Lexicon (a 
mapping table of term -> termID) in memory. 
▪
This greatly compresses the data stream, making the subsequent 
sorting phase much faster.

text:
Size of the parsed output (before sorting)
▪Huge space is needed for large collections, even using the 
most optimized output: 
      <termID, docID, docFreq> 
for each non-positional posting entry
▪Suppose to use 12 bytes (3 integers of 4 Bytes each)
▪T = 100,000,000 are the non-positional postings for the RCV1 
corpus
▪100M * 12B = 1.2 GB 
▪Nowadays we can sort this collection in memory thanks to the 
large size of RAM, but real collections are much larger than

text:
In-memory sorting
▪In-Memory Sorting Limit: Sorting massive datasets entirely in 
RAM is technically unfeasible.
▪The Virtual Memory Trap: The OS allows us to allocate massive 
arrays, much larger than RAM’s size, exploiting Virtual memory
▪Good if you access the data structure sequentially, with high spatial locality
▪The Page Fault Bottleneck: Sorting requires random memory 
access. 
Attempting to sort this virtual array triggers excessive page faults, 
resulting in catastrophic performance drops as the system 
constantly reads/writes to the disk.
To sort big data, we need to split data and reasoning in terms of 
memory blocks (much smaller than RAM’s size)

text:
BSBI: Blocked sort-based Indexing
(Sorting with fewer disk seeks, out-of-core algorithm exploiting 
spatial locality)
▪12-byte (4+4+4) records <termID, docID, docFreq>
▪generated as we parse docs.
▪For RCV1, we should sort T=100M records by term-ID
▪Define a Block ~ 10M of such records (size=120 MB)
▪Can easily fit a couple into memory.
▪Will have 10 such blocks.
▪Basic idea of algorithm:
▪Accumulate postings for each block until its capacity is 
reached, sort in memory the block, write to disk the 
intermediate results.
▪Then, merge the blocks into one long sorted order.

text:
How to merge the sorted runs?
▪Rather than binary merges, it is more efficient to do 
a multi-way merge, (or k-way merge) where we read 
from all sorted blocks
▪Out-of-memory merge
▪Aimed to limit the I/O operations, with explicit read/write 
block-based I/O operations
▪Read suitable chunks of each block into memory and then 
write out a suitable output chunk (globally ordered)
▪chunks small enough to be stored in main memory 
▪N+1 chunks, if N are the blocks to be merged 
▪+1 is output chunk

Single-pass in-memory indexing
▪Key idea 1: Generate separate dictionaries for each 
block – no need to maintain term-termID mapping 
across blocks.
▪Key idea 2: Don’t sort. Accumulate postings in 
postings lists as they occur (appending)
▪DocIDs are generated and assigned sequentially to 
documents
▪With these two ideas we can generate a complete 
inverted index for each block.
▪These separate indexes can then be merged into one 
big index.

text:
Sharding is a technique used in database systems and search engines 
to distribute/partition data (index) across multiple physical nodes
▪Why Sharding?
▪Scalability: As the document collection grows, a single machine 
can't handle the load. Sharding distributes the data, allowing the 
search engine to scale horizontally by adding more nodes, e.g., 
MapReduce framework
▪Performance: By distributing the data across multiple nodes, 
sharding can significantly reduce the load on any single server, 
resulting in faster query execution and improved overall system 
performance.
▪Fault Tolerance: Replicating shards enhances fault tolerance. If 
one node fails, the data is still available on replicas deployed on 
other nodes.
Sharding in Modern Search Engines

text:
Term-based partitioning
▪Build a global index on the entire page collection
▪Assign subsets of terms and the corresponding inverted 
lists in the global index to the nodes 
▪Constructing a term-partitioned index requires 
coordination and communication among the nodes
▪Query processing requires to access only a few nodes, 
those storing the relevant posting lists
▪Issue: load imbalance
▪An inverted list can be accessed with potentially fewer disk

text:
Lossless vs. lossy compression
▪Lossless compression: All information is preserved.
▪What we mostly do in IR in the index, after discarding 
information anyway to generate the index 
▪Lossy compression: Discard some information
▪Several of the preprocessing steps can be viewed as lossy 
compression: case folding, stop words, stemming, and 
number elimination
▪Chap 7: Prune postings entries that are unlikely to turn 
up in the top k list for any query.
▪Almost no loss quality for top k list.

text:
Heaps’ Law
 Vocabulary size vs. Collection size
▪Heaps’ law:   M = kTb
▪M = size of the vocabulary
▪T = number of tokens in the collection (collection size)
▪Typical values: 30 ≤ k ≤ 100 and b ≈ 0.5
▪In a log-log plot of M as a function of T, Heaps’ law 
predicts a line with slope about 0.5
▪Linear relationship between the two in the log-log space
▪An empirical finding (“empirical law”)
Sec. 5.1
log M = log kTb
log M = log k + log Tb
log M = log k + b log T
Y = b X  + log k 
Y: log M
X: log T

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Heaps’ law gives the vocabulary size in collections.
▪Zipf’s law studies the relative frequencies of terms
▪thus, impacting the size of the (positional) postings lists
▪In natural language, there are a few very frequent terms 
and very many very rare terms
▪But what are the relationships between the frequencies, 
or the distribution of the frequencies
Sec. 5.1
13
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Assume terms are ranked, from the most frequent to 
the least frequent
▪ti  is the term at rank i in the collection 
▪cfi is collection frequency: the number of occurrences 
of the term ti (at rank i) in the collection 
dfi is the document frequency: the total number of 
documents that contain the term ti in the corpus
tfi,d is the term frequency: The total number of 
occurrences of the term ti in the document d
Sec. 5.1
14
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Zipf’s law: The i-th most frequent term has a 
collection frequency cfi proportional to 1/i 
▪The amount of the i-th most frequent term is 
inversely proportional to its rank i
▪cfi ∝ 1/i    indeed, frequency equal to K/i, where K is 
a normalizing constant, i.e., the value of cf1 
▪K = cf1
▪Equivalently, the product of the frequency cfi of a 
term and its rank i is a constant K
Sec. 5.1
15
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Zipf consequences
▪If the 1st most frequent term (the) occurs cf1 times 
▪then the 2nd  most frequent term (of) occurs cf1/2 times
▪the 3rd most frequent term (and) occurs cf1/3 times … 
▪cfi = K / i =  K  i-1 (i * cfi = K)
▪Linear relationship (slope -1) between log cfi and log i
Sec. 5.1
16
log cfi = log k  i-1
log cfi = log k  + log  i-1
log cfi = log k  - log  i
Y = - X  + log k 
Y: log cfi
X: log i
i : rank

POSTINGS COMPRESSION

text:
Postings compression
▪The postings file is much larger than the dictionary, 
factor of at least 10.
▪Key desideratum: store each posting compactly.
▪A posting for our purposes is a docID.
▪For Reuters (800,000 documents), we would use 32 
bits per docID when using 4-byte integers.
▪Alternatively, we can use log2 800,000 ≈ 20 bits per 
docID.
▪Our goal: use far fewer than 20 bits per docID.
Sec. 5.3
35
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Postings: two conflicting forces
▪A term like arachnocentric occurs in maybe one doc 
out of a million – we would like to store this posting 
using log2 1M ~ 20 bits.
▪A term like the occurs in virtually every doc, so 20 
bits/posting is too expensive.
▪Prefer 0/1 bitmap vector in this case 
Sec. 5.3
36
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Postings file entry
▪We store the list of docs containing a term in 
increasing order of docID.
▪computer: 33,47,154,159,202 …
▪Consequence: it suffices to store gaps (still delta 
encoding)
▪33,14,107,5,43 …
▪Hope: most gaps can be encoded/stored with far 
fewer than 20 bits.
Sec. 5.3
37
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Three postings' entries
Sec. 5.3
38
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Variable length encoding
▪Aim:
▪For arachnocentric, we will use ~20 bits/gap entry.
▪For the, we will use ~1 bit/gap entry.
▪If the average gap for a term is G, we want to use 
~log2G bits per each gap entry
▪Indeed, log2 𝐺+ 1 bits are needed
▪Key challenge: encode every integer (gap) with about 
as few bits as needed for that integer.
▪This requires a variable length encoding
▪Variable length codes achieve this by using short 
codes for small numbers
Sec. 5.3
39
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Variable Byte (VB) codes
(byte aligned code)
▪For a gap value G, we want to use close to the fewest 
bytes needed to hold log2 G bits
▪Begin with one byte to store G and dedicate 1 bit in it 
to be a continuation bit c
▪If G ≤127
▪binary-encode it in the 7 available bits and set c =1
▪If G >127 
▪Encode the G’s highest-order 7 bits and then use additional 
bytes to encode the lower order bits using the same 
algorithm ← pad leadings zeroes to represent numbers with 
a bit number that is a multiple of 7  
▪at the end, set c =1 the bytes containing the lowest 7 bits – 
and for the other bytes c = 0.
Sec. 5.3
40
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Example
docIDs
824 
829 
215406
gaps
5
214577
VB code
00000110 
10111000 
10000101 
00001101 
00001100 
10110001
Postings stored as the byte concatenation
000001101011100010000101000011010000110010110001
Key property: VB-encoded postings are uniquely 
prefix-decodable.
No whole code word in the system is a prefix of any other 
code word (no special marker is needed to separate codes).
For a small gap (5), VB uses a whole byte.
Sec. 5.3
41
11001110002 = 82410
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Other variable unit codes
▪Instead of bytes, we can also use a different “unit of 
alignment”: 32 bits (words), 16 bits, 4 bits (nibbles).
▪Variable byte alignment wastes space if you have 
many small gaps – nibbles do better in such cases.
▪Variable byte codes:
▪Used by many commercial/research systems
▪Good low-tech blend of variable-length coding and 
sensitivity to computer memory alignment matches (vs. 
bit-level codes, which we look at next).
▪There is also recent work on word-aligned codes that 
pack a variable number of gaps into one word
Sec. 5.3
42
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Unary code
▪Represent n as n 1’s with a final 0.
▪Unary code for 3 is 1110.
▪Unary code for 40 is
11111111111111111111111111111111111111110 
▪Unary code for 80 is:
11111111111111111111111111111111111111111111
1111111111111111111111111111111111110
▪This doesn't look promising, but….
43
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
(Elias) Gamma codes
▪We can compress better with bit-level codes
▪The Gamma code is the best known of these.
▪Represent a gap G as a pair length and offset
▪The length refers to the # of bits used to represent a gap
▪offset is G in binary, with the leading bit cut off
▪For example: 13 → 1101 → 101
▪length is the length of offset
▪For 13 (offset 101), this is 3.
▪We encode length with unary code: 1110.
▪Gamma code of 13 is the concatenation of length 
and offset: 1110101
Sec. 5.3
44
Leading 1 is implicit

text:
Gamma code properties
▪Almost within a factor of 2 of best possible:  log2 𝐺+1
▪G is encoded using   2 log2 𝐺+ 1 bits
▪Length of offset is log2 𝐺 bits (1 less than the number of 
bits to represent G)
▪Length of length is log2 𝐺+ 1 bits (included the ending 0)
▪All gamma codes have an odd number of bits
▪Gamma code can be used for any distribution of 
integers
▪Gamma code is parameter-free

text:
Gamma generates a prefix-free code 
to encode integers
▪Gamma code is uniquely prefix-decodable
▪“prefix free” property: no code that is a prefix of other code
▪The Huffmann code in a famous prefix-free code
▪a symbol-by-symbol encoding with a known input probability 
distribution
▪each code is uniquely identified by a path of a binary tree

Why not use exactly log2 𝐺+ 1 bits? 
▪List of GAPs:     2, 4, 2, 7, 8, 5
1  0  1  0  0  1  0  1  1  1  1  0  0  0  1  0  1
▪The encoding is not “prefix free” : no code that is 
a prefix of other code
▪The encoding of 2 is a prefix of the encoding of 4 and 8
▪Since the “prefix free” property doesn’t hold, the bit string 
can be interpreted in different ways when decoding (no 
unique interpretation)

text:
Gamma seldom used in practice
▪Machines have byte/word boundaries – 8, 16, 32, 64 bits
▪Operations that cross word boundaries are slower
▪Compressing and manipulating at the granularity of bits 
can be slow
▪Variable byte encoding is aligned and thus potentially 
more efficient
▪Regardless of efficiency, variable byte is conceptually 
simpler at little additional space cost
▪Finally, if gaps are large, VB is better
▪For a number G,  VB:
log 𝐺+1

text:
Elias Delta vs. Elias Gamma
▪γ-codes are relatively inefficient for large numbers 
▪length of offset → inefficient unary code. 
▪δ-codes encode the length in γ-code instead of unary 
code, whereas the encoding of offset is the same
▪For example:
▪the δ-code of 7 is 10,0,11 (we add commas for readability).
▪10,0 is the γ-code for length (2 in this case) and the
▪encoding of offset (11) is unchanged
▪δ-encoding thus uses 
▪2 ∗log 𝐿+ 1  bits to represent a length 𝐿= log 𝐺,  
rather than 𝐿+1 bits as γ-encoding does by using unary code

Other methods: Golomb-Rice
▪Choose an integer M = 2j , the modulus
▪Golomb-Rice is NOT parameter-free 
▪We want to encode the integer n > 0. 
▪Split n into two integer components, the quotient q(n) 
and the remainder r(n): 
▪q 𝑛=
𝑛−1
𝑀
▪r 𝑛= 𝑛−1 𝑚𝑜𝑑 𝑀
▪Encode n by writing: 
▪q 𝑛 in unary
▪followed by r 𝑛  as a binary number on j bits 
▪0 ≦𝑟𝑛< 𝑀  represented on 𝐥𝐨𝐠𝟐𝑴= 𝒋 bits  
▪Note that for representing M = 2j  we should need j+1 bits

Other methods: Golomb-Rice

the shorter the representation of 𝑞
▪𝑞 is more critical,  as it is represented in unary
▪For this reason, 𝑗 is chosen in such a way that 2j is 
concentrated around the mean of the elements (e.g., 
the gaps) to be represented

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪PForDelta aims to encode an integer sequence, instead of 
representing each single integer separately
▪The idea is to split the sequence into blocks of fixed-memory 
size and determines how many integers can be packed in a unit. 
▪PForDelta has an effective strategy to deal with possible 
large values for which more bits are needed
▪In the end, blocks contains splits of integer, all 
represented by the same amount of bits
▪This enables efficient decompression, also using SIMD parallel 
computation on packed data.
57
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪Assume most of integers to compress fall in the interval I:
 
 [base,  base + 2k - 1] 
▪Translate the values in the new interval, subtracting base 
to each value:
 
[0,  2k - 1]
▪Values outside I are called exceptions 
▪represented in the compressed list with an escape symbol
▪encoded explicitly in a separate list using a fixed-size 
representation of w bits (e.g., a whole memory word)
▪To mark the presence of an exception, we need to encode the 
escape symbol, thus we have [0,  2k − 2] available configurations
▪k chosen to allow a large part of the block to be represented 
w/o exceptions (with only k bits)
58

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪Example:   
[3, 4, 7, 21, 9, 12, 2, 17] 
▪Parameters:
▪base = 2 and k = 4 (interval I: [base,  base + 2k - 2] = [2, 16])
▪Special escape symbol * marks the presence of an exception, 
represented by:   2k - 1 = 11112 = 1510, 
▪(Translated) Values between from 0 to 24 - 2 = 1410  can be 
represented with k=4 bits (-2 because one configuration of 
the 4 bits is used for the escape symbol)
▪Translation after subtracting base and Representation:
  
[1, 2, 5, ∗, 7, 10, 2, ∗] − [21, 17]
▪Exceptions are written in a separate sequence, here reported 
after the dash

Other methods: PForDelta
▪Compressed:
  
[1, 2, 5, ∗, 7, 10, 0, ∗] − [21, 17]
▪PForDelta encodes blocks of B consecutive integers stored 
in a multi-word – thus word aligned block of memory
▪i.e., multiple of 32 bits 
▪e.g, if k=6,   LCM (6, 32) = 96 bits = 3 words,   B = 96/6 = 16 ints
▪The exceptions and stored in another array
▪merged to the original sequence of codewords during the 
decoding phase (thus paying w+k bits for each exception)

text:
Other methods: Interpolative Coding
▪A completely different approach is taken by Binary 
Interpolative Coding
▪No gaps (delta-encoding step)
▪directly encodes strictly monotone sequences
▪effectively exploits burst of close integers being encoded 
▪This method recursively splits the sequence of integers in 
two halves
▪It encodes at each split the middle element and recursively the 
two halves
▪Intuition of the stop condition of the recursion:
▪If I say that the sorted sequence to encode goes from 100 to 120, 
and its length is 21, its is enough to derive that the sequence is 
[100, 101, ….., 119, 120], where all gaps are equal to 1 
62
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Other methods: Interpolative Coding
▪First, we encode:
▪Length, First and Last element, using for example Gamma
▪A trick to save bits:
▪Rather than encoding (big) numbers, the method encodes  
positions in integer ranges
▪At each recursive step, the range that encloses the middle 
element is reduced, and so is the number of bits needed 
to encode it
63

Index compression summary
▪We can now create an index for highly efficient 
Boolean retrieval that is very space efficient
▪Only 4% of the total size of the collection
▪Only 10-15% of the total size of the text in the 
collection
▪However, we’ve ignored positional information
▪Hence, space savings are less for indexes used in practice
▪But techniques substantially the same.

text:
Some information theory
▪Prefix-free encoding for an integer alphabet (gaps) 
▪Shannon showed that: 
▪if we know that a random variable X takes values with a discrete 
probability distribution: P = p1, . . . , pn
▪the minimum expected message length we can achieve with any 
binary prefix-free code is between H(P) and H(P) + 1, where H(P) 
is the entropy:
▪In conclusion, if the encoding's average bit length is close to 
H(P), it's near-optimal.
68
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Some theory
▪Huffman encoding is known to be optimal w.r.t. the 
Shannon theory
▪Huffman algorithm to encode integers
▪Entropy-based encoding: more common symbols are 
represented using fewer bits than less common ones
▪It works for every probability distribution of integers
▪Drawback: decoding Huffman codes requires traversing a 
tree, or slow table lookups
▪In our case, too large alphabet (natural numbers) to 
apply Huffman
69
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression.pdf

text:
Some information theory
▪Is Elias Gamma optimal wrt H(P) ?
▪When the integers to be compressed conform to the following 
probability distribution: 
▪Pr(n) = 1 / (2 n 2) = 0.5 n-2
▪Pr(1)=1/2     Pr(2)=1/8     Pr(3)=1/18      Pr(4)=1/32 …….
▪The distribution of numbers follows a kind of power-law, and it
is skewed towards small values (a few large numbers)

text:
Lossless vs. lossy compression
▪Lossless compression: All information is preserved.
▪What we mostly do in classical IR to compress the inverted 
index
▪Although information is lost during the preprocessing steps 
required to build the index
▪e.g., documents are represented as sets of terms for a pure 
Boolean IR model
▪Lossy compression: Discard some information
▪Several of the preprocessing steps can be viewed as lossy 
compression: case folding, stop words, stemming, discarding 
information about  term frequency in each document
Sec. 5.1
8

text:
Vocabulary vs. collection size
▪How big is the term vocabulary?
▪That is, how many distinct words are there?
▪Can we assume an upper bound? Not really.
▪At least 7020 = 1037 different words of length 20, considering 
an alphabet of 70 printable symbols
▪It’s a gross overestimated bound
▪If we consider Unicode, the distinct symbols to consider 
keep growing ☺
▪In practice, we will discover that vocabulary keep 
growing with collection size according to a specific law

text:
Heaps’ Law
Vocabulary size vs. Collection size
▪Heaps’ law:   M = kTb
▪M = size of the vocabulary
▪T = number of tokens in the collection (collection size)
▪Typical values: 30 ≤ k ≤ 100 and b ≈ 0.5
▪In a log-log plot of M as a function of T, Heaps’ law 
predicts a line with slope about 0.5
▪Linear relationship between the two in the log-log space
▪An empirical finding (“empirical law”)
Sec. 5.1

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Heaps’ law gives the vocabulary size in collections.
▪Zipf’s law studies the relative frequencies of terms
▪thus, impacting the size of the (positional) postings lists
▪In natural language, there are a few very frequent terms 
and very many very rare terms
▪But what are the relationships between the frequencies, or the 
distribution of the frequencies

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Assume terms are ranked, from the most frequent to 
the least frequent
▪ti is the term at rank i in the collection 
▪cfi is collection frequency: the number of occurrences 
of the term ti (at rank i) in the collection 
dfi is the document frequency: the total number of 
documents that contain the term ti in the corpus
tfi,d is the term frequency: The total number of 
occurrences of the term ti in the document d

dfi is the document frequency: the total number of

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Zipf’s law: The i-th most frequent term ti has a 
collection frequency cfi proportional to 1/i
▪cfi ∝1/i
▪the amount of the i-th most frequent term ti is 
inversely proportional to its rank i
▪Indeed, cfi equal to K/i, where K is a normalizing 
constant, i.e., the value of cf1
▪K = cf1
▪Equivalently, the product of the frequency cfi of a 
term and its rank i is a constant K
Sec. 5.1
16
proportional to size of the collection.
[NOTE] proportional to size of the collection.

text:
Zipf consequences
▪If the 1st most frequent term (the) occurs cf1 times 
▪then the 2nd most frequent term (of) occurs cf1/2 times
▪the 3rd most frequent term (and) occurs cf1/3 times … 
▪cfi = K / i =  K  i-1
(i * cfi = K)
▪Linear relationship (slope -1) between log cfi and log i
Sec. 5.1
17
log cfi = log k  i-1
log cfi = log k  + log  i-1

▪Aim:

▪This requires a variable length encoding, then using

text:
Variable Byte (VB) codes
(byte aligned code)
▪Begin with one byte to store G and dedicate 1 bit in it 
to be a continuation bit c
▪If G ≤127
▪binary-encode it in the 7 available bits and set c =1
▪If G >127 
▪Encode the G’s highest-order 7 bits and then use additional 
bytes to encode the lower order bits using the same 
algorithm ← pad leadings zeroes to represent numbers with 
a bit number that is a multiple of 7  
▪at the end, set c =1 the bytes containing the lowest 7 bits –
and for the other bytes c = 0.

text:
Other variable unit codes
▪Instead of bytes, we can also use a different “unit of 
alignment”: 32 bits (words), 16 bits, 4 bits (nibbles).
▪Variable byte alignment wastes space if you have 
many small gaps – nibbles do better in such cases.
▪Variable byte codes:
▪Used by many commercial/research systems
▪Good low-tech blend of variable-length coding and 
sensitivity to computer memory alignment matches (vs. 
bit-level codes, which we look at next).
▪There is also recent work on word-aligned codes that 
pack a variable number of gaps into one word

text:
(Elias) Gamma codes
▪We can compress better with bit-level codes
▪The Gamma code is the best known of these.
▪Represent a gap G as a pair length and offset
▪The length refers to the # of bits used to represent a gap
▪offset is G in binary, with the leading bit cut off
▪For example: 13 → 1101 → 101
▪length is the length of offset
▪For 13 (offset 101), this is 3.
▪We encode length with unary code: 1110.
▪Gamma code of 13 is the concatenation of length
and offset: 1110101

text:
Gamma seldom used in practice
▪Machines have byte/word boundaries – 8, 16, 32, 64 bits
▪Operations that cross word boundaries are slower
▪Compressing and manipulating at the granularity of bits 
can be slow
▪Variable byte encoding is aligned and thus potentially 
more efficient
▪Regardless of efficiency, variable byte is conceptually 
simpler at little additional space cost
▪Finally, if gaps are large, VB is better

text:
Elias Delta vs. Elias Gamma
▪γ-codes are relatively inefficient for large numbers
▪length of offset → inefficient unary code. 
▪δ-codes encode the length in γ-code instead of unary 
code, whereas the encoding of offset is the same
▪For example:
▪the δ-code of 7 is 10,0,11 (we add commas for readability).
▪10,0 is the γ-code for length (2 in this case) and the
▪encoding of offset (11) is unchanged
▪δ-encoding thus uses 
▪2 ∗log 𝐿+ 1 bits to represent a length 𝐿= log 𝐺,  
rather than 𝐿+1 bits as γ-encoding does by using unary code

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪PForDelta aims to encode an integer sequence, instead of 
representing each single integer separately
▪The idea is to split the sequence into blocks of fixed-memory 
size and determines how many integers can be packed in a unit. 
▪PForDelta has an effective strategy to deal with possible 
large values for which more bits are needed
▪In the end, blocks contains splits of integer, all 
represented by the same amount of bits
▪This enables efficient decompression, also using SIMD parallel 
computation on packed data.
58
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression2.pdf

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪Assume most of integers to compress fall in the interval I:
[base,  base + 2k - 1] 
▪Translate the values in the new interval, subtracting base
to each value:
[0,  2k - 1]
▪Values outside I are called exceptions
▪represented in the compressed list with an escape symbol
▪encoded explicitly in a separate list using a fixed-size 
representation of w bits (e.g., a whole memory word)
▪To mark the presence of an exception, we need to encode the 
escape symbol, thus we have [0,  2k − 2] available configurations
▪k chosen to allow a large part of the block to be represented 
w/o exceptions (with only k bits)
59

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪Example:  
[3, 4, 7, 21, 9, 12, 2, 17] 
▪Parameters:
▪base = 2 and k = 4 (interval I: [base,  base + 2k - 2] = [2, 16])
▪Special escape symbol * marks the presence of an exception, 
represented by:   2k - 1 = 11112 = 1510, 
▪(Translated) Values between from 0 to 24 - 2 = 1410 can be 
represented with k=4 bits (-2 because one configuration of 
the 4 bits is used for the escape symbol)
▪Translation after subtracting base and Representation:
[1, 2, 5, ∗, 7, 10, 2, ∗] − [21, 17]
▪Exceptions are written in a separate sequence, here reported 
after the dash

Other methods: PForDelta
▪Compressed:
[1, 2, 5, ∗, 7, 10, 0, ∗] − [21, 17]
▪PForDelta encodes blocks of B consecutive integers stored 
in a multi-word – thus word aligned block of memory
▪i.e., multiple of 32 bits 
▪e.g, if k=6,   LCM (6, 32) = 96 bits = 3 words,   B = 96/6 = 16 ints
▪The exceptions and stored in another array
▪merged to the original sequence of codewords during the 
decoding phase (thus paying w+k bits for each exception)

text:
Other methods: Interpolative Coding
▪A completely different approach is taken by Binary 
Interpolative Coding
▪No gaps (delta-encoding step)
▪directly encodes strictly monotone sequences
▪effectively exploits burst of close integers being encoded 
▪This method recursively splits the sequence of integers in 
two halves
▪It encodes at each split the middle element and recursively the 
two halves
▪Intuition of the stop condition of the recursion:
▪If I say that the sorted sequence to encode goes from 100 to 120, 
and its length is 21, its is enough to derive that the sequence is 
[100, 101, ….., 119, 120], where all gaps are equal to 1

▪A trick to save bits:
▪Rather than encoding (big) numbers, the method encodes  
positions in integer ranges
▪At each recursive step, the range that encloses the middle 
element is reduced, and so is the number of bits needed 
to encode it

▪We can now create an index for highly efficient 
Boolean retrieval that is very space efficient

text:
Some information theory
▪Prefix-free encoding for an integer alphabet (gaps) 
▪Shannon showed that: 
▪if we know that a random variable X takes values with a discrete 
probability distribution: P = p1, . . . , pn
▪the minimum expected message length we can achieve with any 
binary prefix-free code is between H(P) and H(P) + 1, where H(P) 
is the entropy:
▪In conclusion, if the encoding's average bit length is close to 
H(P), it's near-optimal.

text:
Some theory
▪Huffman encoding is known to be optimal w.r.t. the 
Shannon theory
▪Huffman algorithm to encode integers
▪Entropy-based encoding: more common symbols are 
represented using fewer bits than less common ones
▪It works for every probability distribution of integers
▪Drawback: decoding Huffman codes requires traversing a 
tree, or slow table lookups
▪In our case, too large alphabet (natural numbers) to 
apply Huffman

text:
Lossless vs. lossy compression
▪Lossless compression: All information is preserved.
▪What we mostly do in classical IR to compress the inverted 
index
▪Although information is lost during the preprocessing steps 
required to build the index
▪e.g., documents are represented as sets of terms for a pure 
Boolean IR model
▪Lossy compression: Discard some information
▪Several of the preprocessing steps can be viewed as lossy 
compression: case folding, stop words, stemming, discarding 
information about  term frequency in each document
Sec. 5.1
8

text:
Vocabulary vs. collection size
▪How big is the term vocabulary?
▪That is, how many distinct words are there?
▪Can we assume an upper bound? Not really.
▪At least 7020 = 1037 different words of length 20, considering 
an alphabet of 70 printable symbols
▪It’s a gross overestimated bound
▪If we consider Unicode, the distinct symbols to consider 
keep growing ☺
▪In practice, we will discover that vocabulary keep 
growing with collection size according to a specific law
Sec. 5.1

text:
Heaps’ Law
Vocabulary size vs. Collection size
▪Heaps’ law:   M = kTb
▪M = size of the vocabulary
▪T = number of tokens in the collection (collection size)
▪Typical values: 30 ≤ k ≤ 100 and b ≈ 0.5
▪In a log-log plot of M as a function of T, Heaps’ law 
predicts a line with slope about 0.5
▪Linear relationship between the two in the log-log space
▪An empirical finding (“empirical law”)
Sec. 5.1
log M = log kTb
log M = log k + log Tb
log M = log k + b log T
Y = b X  + log k 
Y: log M
X: log T

dfi is the document frequency: the total number of 
documents that contain the term ti in the corpus

occurrences of the term ti in the document d

text:
Zipf’s law
▪Zipf’s law: The i-th most frequent term ti has a 
collection frequency cfi proportional to 1/i
▪cfi ∝1/i
▪the amount of the i-th most frequent term ti is 
inversely proportional to its rank i
▪Indeed, cfi equal to K/i, where K is a normalizing 
constant, i.e., the value of cf1
▪K = cf1
▪Equivalently, the product of the frequency cfi of a 
term and its rank i is a constant K
Sec. 5.1
16
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression3.pdf

text:
Zipf consequences
▪If the 1st most frequent term (the) occurs cf1 times 
▪then the 2nd most frequent term (of) occurs cf1/2 times
▪the 3rd most frequent term (and) occurs cf1/3 times … 
▪cfi = K / i =  K  i-1
(i * cfi = K)
▪Linear relationship (slope -1) between log cfi and log i
Sec. 5.1

text:
Postings compression
▪The postings file is much larger than the dictionary, 
factor of at least 10.
▪Key desideratum: store each posting compactly.
▪A posting for our purposes is a docID.
▪For Reuters (800,000 documents), we would use 32 
bits per docID when using 4-byte integers.
▪Alternatively, we can use log2 800,000 ≈ 20 bits per 
docID.
▪Our goal: use far fewer than 20 bits per docID.
Sec. 5.3
36
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression3.pdf

text:
Postings: two conflicting forces
▪A term like arachnocentric occurs in maybe one doc 
out of a million – we would like to store this posting 
using log2 1M ~ 20 bits.
▪A term like the occurs in virtually every doc, so 20 
bits/posting is too expensive.
▪Prefer 0/1 bitmap vector in this case

text:
Variable length encoding
▪Aim:
▪For arachnocentric: to use ~20 bits per gap
▪For the: to use ~1 bit per gap
▪Key challenge: encode every integer (gap) with about 
as few bits as needed for that integer.
▪If the average gap for a term is G, we want to use ~log2G
bits per each gap entry
▪Indeed, 𝐥𝐨𝐠𝟐𝑮+ 𝟏bits are needed
▪This requires a variable length encoding, then using 
very short codes for very small numbers
Sec. 5.3
40
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture5-compression3.pdf

text:
Variable Byte (VB) codes
(byte aligned code)
▪Begin with one byte to store G and dedicate 1 bit in it 
to be a continuation bit c
▪If G ≤127
▪binary-encode it in the 7 available bits and set c =1
▪If G >127 
▪Encode the G’s highest-order 7 bits and then use additional 
bytes to encode the lower order bits using the same 
algorithm ← pad leadings zeroes to represent numbers with 
a bit number that is a multiple of 7  
▪at the end, set c =1 the bytes containing the lowest 7 bits –
and for the other bytes c = 0.
Sec. 5.3

text:
Other variable unit codes
▪Instead of bytes, we can also use a different “unit of 
alignment”: 32 bits (words), 16 bits, 4 bits (nibbles).
▪Variable byte alignment wastes space if you have 
many small gaps – nibbles do better in such cases.
▪Variable byte codes:
▪Used by many commercial/research systems
▪Good low-tech blend of variable-length coding and 
sensitivity to computer memory alignment matches (vs. 
bit-level codes, which we look at next).
▪There is also recent work on word-aligned codes that 
pack a variable number of gaps into one word
Sec. 5.3

text:
(Elias) Gamma codes
▪We can compress better with bit-level codes
▪The Gamma code is the best known of these.
▪Represent a gap G as a pair length and offset
▪The length refers to the # of bits used to represent a gap
▪offset is G in binary, with the leading bit truncated
▪For example: 13 → 1101 → 101
▪length is the length of offset
▪For 13 (offset 101), this is 3.
▪We encode length by unary code: 1110.
▪Gamma code of 13 is the concatenation of length
and offset: 1110101

text:
Gamma code properties
▪Almost within a factor of 2 of best possible:  log2 𝐺+1
▪G is encoded using   2 log2 𝐺+ 1 bits
▪Length of offset is log2 𝐺bits (1 less than the number of bits 
to represent G)
▪Length of length is log2 𝐺+ 1 bits (included the ending 0)
▪All gamma codes have an odd number of bits
▪Gamma code can be used for any distribution of integers
▪Gamma code is parameter-free
▪Gamma works better if very small gaps are very frequent

text:
Gamma generates a prefix-free code to 
encode integers, like Huffmann encoding
▪Gamma code is uniquely prefix-decodable
▪“prefix free” property: no code is a prefix of other code
▪The Huffmann code in a famous prefix-free code
▪a symbol-by-symbol encoding with a known input probability
distribution
More frequent symbols → shorter codes
Less frequent symbols → longer codes

encode integers, like Huffmann encoding
▪The optimal Huffman’s algorithm builds a binary tree by:
▪Repeatedly merging the two least probable symbols. 
▪Assigning longer codes to rare symbols. 
▪Assigning shorter codes to frequent symbols.

text:
Gamma seldom used in practice
▪Machines have byte/word boundaries – 8, 16, 32, 64 bits
▪Operations that cross word boundaries are slower
▪Compressing and manipulating at the granularity of bits 
can be slow
▪Variable byte encoding is aligned and thus potentially 
more efficient
▪Regardless of efficiency, variable byte is conceptually 
simpler at little additional space cost
▪Finally, if gaps are large, VB is better
▪For a number G,  VB:

text:
Other methods: PForDelta
▪PForDelta (Patched Frame-of-Reference with Delta 
encoding) aims to encode an integer sequence, instead of 
representing each single integer separately
▪The idea is to split the sequence into blocks
▪In each block, all integers/gaps are represented by the same 
amount of bits
▪This also determines how many integers can be packed in a 
block of a given size
▪PForDelta enables efficient decompression, also using SIMD 
parallel computation on packed data
▪PForDelta has an effective strategy to deal with possible 
large values for which more bits are needed: patching 61

text:
PForDelta
▪How to represent exceptions (values outside I)?
▪We need to represent an escape symbol in the compressed list
▪Then, exceptions are encoded in a separate list using a fixed-size 
representation of w bits (e.g., a whole memory word)
▪To encode the escape symbol
▪We assume that most of the integers to compress fall in the 
interval I’:   [base,  base + 2k - 2] 
▪Thus, we have [0,  2k − 2] available configurations of the k bits 
▪escape symbol  = the string of k bits  1…1 is used to encode the 
exception
▪k chosen to allow a large part of the block to be represented 
w/o exceptions

PForDelta

[1, 2, 5, ∗, 7, 10, 0, ∗] − [21, 17]
▪PForDelta encodes blocks of B consecutive integers stored 
in a multi-word – thus word aligned block of memory
▪i.e., multiple of 32 bits = 4 B
▪e.g, if k=6,   LCM (6, 32) = 96 bits = 3 words,   B = 96/6 = 16 ints
▪The exceptions are stored in another array
▪merged to the original sequence of codewords during the 
decoding phase (thus paying w+k bits for each exception)

text:
PForDelta vs. bit-aligned codes
▪Elias/Golomb (variable-length , bit-aligned codes)
▪To decode bit streams, the CPU must read data bit-by-bit, using 
expensive bit-shifting/masking operations, and unpredictable if
statements 
▪PForDELTA (integers of the same-length, packed in blocks
of memory - one/multiple words)
▪faster at decoding time than bit-aligned codes
▪Modern CPUs are heavily optimized to read word blocks at once
▪Every number in the block takes exactly k bits: to decompress we 
don’t need any if statements, except for dealing with exception
▪PForDelta is perfectly suited for SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple 
Data) operations (using SSE or AVX instructions)
▪PForDELTA: two order of magnitude faster than bit-aligned 
decoding, but slightly worse for compression rate
