text:
Other methods: Interpolative Coding
▪A completely different approach is taken by Binary 
Interpolative Coding
▪No gaps (no delta-encoding step)
▪directly encodes strictly monotone sequences
▪effectively exploits burst of close integers being encoded 
▪This method recursively splits a sequence into two halves
▪It encodes at each split the middle element and recursively 
handles the two halves
▪We assume to know at each step of encoding/deconding: min, 
max, len of the sequence
▪Intuition of the stop condition of the recursion:
▪If I say that the sorted sequence to encode goes from 100 to 120, 
and its length is 21, its is enough to conclude that the sequence 
is [100, 101, ….., 119, 120], where all gaps are equal to 1

text:
Interpolative Coding
▪First, we encode:
▪Length, First and Last element, using for example Gamma
▪A trick to save bits:
▪Rather than encoding (big) numbers, the method encodes  
positions in integer ranges
▪At each recursive step, the range that encloses the middle 
element is reduced, and so is the number of bits needed to 
encode it
▪Interpolative Coding is recursive (implicit binary tree)
▪the midpoints are not stored in the sequential order they
appear in the posting list (e.g., left-to-right). 
▪Instead, they are stored sequentially in the bitstream using a 
pre-order traversal (Root, Left, Right) of the implicit tree created
by the recursive splits.

text:
Interpolative Coding
▪Example: a sequence of 7 docISs [3, 5, 8, 12, 15, 18, 21]
▪Entire Array: The midpoint is 12, and the bitstream gets: [bits for 12]
▪Array is split into Left [3, 5, 8] and Right [15, 18, 21].
▪Go Left [3, 5, 8]: The midpoint is 5, and the bitstream gets: [bits for 5]
▪Array is split into Left [3] and Right [8].
▪Go Left [3]: The midpoint is 3, and the bitstream gets: [bits for 3]
▪No elements left on the left or right of 3. Return up.
▪Go Right [8]: The midpoint is 8 and the bitstream gets: [bits for 8]
▪No elements left. Return up. Left side of 12 is now completely finished.
▪Go Right of Root [15, 18, 21]: ……
….
▪Final Storage Order in the Bitstream: The midpoints are stored
consecutively as [12, 5, 3, 8, 18, 15, 21]

Index compression summary
▪We can now create an index for highly efficient 
Boolean retrieval that is very space efficient
▪Only 4% of the total size of the collection
▪Only 10-15% of the total size of the text in the

▪However, we’ve ignored positional information
▪Hence, space savings are less for indexes used in practice
▪But techniques substantially the same.

text:
Some information theory
▪Prefix-free encoding for an integer alphabet (gaps) 
▪Shannon showed that: 
▪if we know that a random variable X takes n values with a 
discrete probability distribution: P = p1, . . . , pn
▪the minimum expected message length we can achieve with any 
binary prefix-free code is between H(P) and H(P) + 1, where H(P) 
is the entropy:
▪In conclusion, if the encoding's average bit length is close to 
H(P), it's near-optimal.

text:
Some theory
▪Huffman encoding is known to be optimal w.r.t. the 
Shannon theory
▪Huffman algorithm to encode integers
▪Entropy-based encoding: more common symbols are 
represented using fewer bits than less common ones
▪It works for every probability distribution of integers
▪Drawback: decoding Huffman codes requires traversing a 
tree, or slow table lookups
▪In our case, too large alphabet (natural numbers) to 
apply Huffman

text:
Some information theory
▪Is Elias Gamma optimal wrt H(P) ?
▪When the integers to be compressed conform to the following 
probability distribution: 
▪Pr(n) = 1 / (2 n 2) = 0.5 n-2
▪Pr(1)=1/2     Pr(2)=1/8     Pr(3)=1/18      Pr(4)=1/32 …….
▪The distribution of numbers should follows a kind of power-law, 
thus skewed towards small values (a few large numbers)

text:
Ranked retrieval
▪So far, our queries have all been Boolean.
▪Documents either match or don’t.
▪Good for expert users with precise understanding of 
their needs and the collection.
▪Also good for applications: Applications can easily consume 
1000s of results.
▪Not good for most users.
▪Incapable of writing Boolean queries (or they are, but they 
think it’s too much work).
▪Don’t want to wade through 1000s of results.
▪This is particularly true of web search.
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Problem with Boolean search:
feast or famine
▪Boolean “conjuntive queries” often result in either 
too few (=0) or too many (1000s) results.
▪Query 1: “standard user dlink 650” → 200,000 hits
▪Query 2: “standard user dlink 650 no card found”: 0 hits
▪It takes a lot of skill to come up with a query that 
produces a manageable number of hits.
▪AND gives too few; OR gives too many
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Ranked retrieval models
▪Rather than a set of documents satisfying a query 
expression, in ranked retrieval, the system reorders 
the (top) documents in the collection for a given 
query
▪Free text queries: Rather than a query language of 
operators and expressions, the user’s query is just 
one or more words in a natural language
▪the user specifies her/is information need through an ad-
hoc query which initiates a search
▪Ranked retrieval has normally been associated with 
free text queries and vice versa
7
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Feast or famine: not a problem in 
ranked retrieval
▪When a system produces a ranked result set, 
large result sets are not an issue
▪Indeed, the size of the result set is not an issue
▪We just show the top k ( ≈ 10) results
▪We don’t overwhelm the user
▪Premise: the ranking algorithm works ☺
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Scoring as the basis of ranked retrieval
▪We wish to return “in order” those documents 
▪First the ones most likely to be useful to users
▪How can we rank-order the documents in the 
collection with respect to a query?
▪Assign a score – say in [0, 1] – to each document 
given a query
▪This score measures how well document and query 
“match”
▪We need a way of assigning a score to a 
query/document pair
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring?
▪Recall: A commonly used measure of overlap of two 
sets A and B
▪jaccard(A,B) = |A ∩B| / |A ∪B|
▪jaccard(A,A) = 1
▪jaccard(A,B) = 0 if A ∩ B = 0
▪A and B don’t have to be of the same size.
▪Always assigns a number between 0 and 1
▪Higher is better
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

▪J(q, d1) = 1 / (3+4-1) = 1/6
▪J(q, d2) = 1 / (3+3-1) = 1/5

text:
Issues with Jaccard for scoring
▪It doesn't consider term frequency (how many times 
a term occurs in a document)
▪Jaccard doesn’t consider this information, as texts are 
represented as “sets” of terms, as in the Boolean model of 
IR 
▪Term frequency
▪The more frequent the query term in the document, the 
higher the score (should be)
▪Rare terms in a collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
Ch. 6

text:
Bag of words model (multiset)
▪Count vector (bag of words) representation doesn't 
consider the ordering of words in a document
▪John is quicker than Mary and Mary is quicker than John
have the same vectors
▪In a sense, this is a step back: The positional index was 
able to distinguish these two documents.
▪We will look at “recovering” positional information later in 
this course.

▪dft is the document frequency: the total number of

▪tft,d is the term frequency: The total number of 
occurrences of term t in a document d

text:
Term frequency tf
▪The term frequency tft,d of term t in document d is 
defined as the count/number of times that t occurs in d
▪We want to use tft,d when computing query-document 
match scores. But how?
▪Raw term frequency is not what we want:
▪A document with 10 occurrences of the term is more relevant 
than a document with 1 occurrence of the term.
▪But not 10 times more relevant
▪Relevance does not increase linearly with term 
frequency
frequency = count in IR

both q and d:

text:
Document frequency
▪Rare terms in the collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
▪Small dft w.r.t. the size N of the document corpus
▪dft ≪N
▪Consider a term in the query that is (globally) rare in 
the collection (e.g., arachnocentric)
▪A document containing this term is very likely to be relevant 
to the query arachnocentric
→ We want higher weights for rare terms like 
arachnocentric
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Document frequency, continued
▪Frequent terms in the collection are less informative 
than rare terms
▪Consider a query term that is frequent enough in the 
collection (e.g., high, increase, line)
▪A document containing such a term is more likely to be 
relevant than a document that doesn’t contain it
▪But it’s not a sure indicator of relevance wrt the user 
information need, as a large part of the documents
contains this query term
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Document frequency, continued
▪We will use document frequency (dft) to capture this
▪we want high positive weights for words like high, 
increase, and line (characterized by a large dft, but still 
much smaller than the collection size)
▪but lower than weights assigned to globally rare terms, 
characterized by a very small dft
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
How do we weight doc terms in the vectors?
▪Here’s the intuition:
▪Terms that appear often in a document (tf) should get high 
weights
▪Terms that appear in many documents (df) should get low 
weights
▪
How do we capture this mathematically?
▪Term frequency
▪Inverse document frequency
The more often a document contains the term “dog”, 
the more likely that the document is “about” dogs.
Words like “the”, “a”, “of” appear in (nearly) all 
documents.

text:
tf-idf weighting
▪The tf-idf weight of a term is the 
product of its tf weight (normalized wf) and its idf weight.
▪Best known weighting scheme in Information Retrieval
▪Note: the “-” in tf-idf is a hyphen, not a minus sign!
▪Alternative names:  tf.idf,   tf x idf
▪Increases with the number of occurrences within a document
▪Increases with the rarity of the term in the collection
Sec. 6.2.2
Alternative smaller:
log(1 + tft,d)

text:
Documents as vectors
▪So, we have a |V|-dimensional vector space
▪Vocabulary’s terms are axes of the space
▪Documents are points or vectors in this space
▪Very high-dimensional: tens of millions of 
dimensions when you apply this to a web search 
engine
▪Very sparse vectors - most entries are zero.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Queries as vectors
▪Key idea 1: Do the same for queries: represent them 
as vectors in the space
▪Key idea 2: Rank documents according to their 
proximity to the query in this vectorial space
▪proximity = similarity of vectors
▪proximity ≈ inverse of distance
▪Recall: We do this because we want to get away from 
the either-in-or-out of the Boolean model.
▪Instead: rank more relevant documents higher than 
less relevant documents
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Formalizing vector space proximity
▪First cut: distance between two points
▪( = distance between the end points of the two vectors)
▪Euclidean distance?
▪Euclidean distance is a bad idea . . .
▪. . . because Euclidean distance is large for vectors of 
different lengths.
▪Euclidean distance works however for normalized 
vectors
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Why Euclidean distance is a bad idea
The Euclidean
distance between q
and d2 is large
although the

in query q and 
document d2 are

Sec. 6.3
Euclidean distance
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Use angle instead of distance
▪Experiment: take a document d and append it to 
itself. Call this document d′
▪d′ and d have the same semantic content
▪Vectors d′ and d overlap, but the magnitudes d′ is double
▪The Euclidean distance between the two documents 
d′ and d can be quite large
▪Although the angle between the two vectorial 
representations of the two documents is 0o (in the vector 
space)
▪Key idea: Rank document vectors according to the 
“angle” with the query vector.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
From angles to cosines
▪The following two notions are equivalent.
▪Rank documents in decreasing order of the angle between 
query and document     angle=distance
▪Rank documents in increasing order  of 
cosine(query,document) 
cosine=similarity
▪Cosine is a monotonically decreasing function in the 
interval [0o, 180o]
▪from 1 (max similarity) to -1 (max dissimilarity)
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

text:
Length normalization
▪A vector can be (length-) normalized by dividing each 
of its components by its length – for this we use the L2
norm:
▪Dividing a vector by its L2 norm makes it a unit (length) 
vector (on the surface of the unit hypersphere)
▪Effect on the two documents d and d′ (d appended to 
itself) from the earlier slide: they have identical vectors 
after length-normalization
▪Long and short documents have now comparable weights
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (1).pdf

di is the tf-idf weight of term i in the document
cos(q,d) is the cosine similarity of q and d … or,
equivalently, the cosine of the angle between q and d.

BM25
▪Okapi BM25 (BM: best matching) is a probabilistic 
ranking function, a state-of-the-art method better

▪Represent the query as a weighted tf-idf vector
▪Represent each document as a weighted tf-idf vector
▪Compute the cosine similarity score for the query 
vector and each document vector
▪Rank documents with respect to the query by score
▪Return the top K (e.g., K = 10) to the user

text:
Ranked retrieval
▪So far, our queries have all been Boolean.
▪Documents either match or don’t.
▪Good for expert users with precise understanding of 
their needs and the collection.
▪Also good for applications: Applications can easily consume 
1000s of results.
▪Not good for most users.
▪Incapable of writing Boolean queries (or they are, but they 
think it’s too much work).
▪Don’t want to wade through 1000s of results.
▪This is particularly true of web search.
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Problem with Boolean search:
feast or famine
▪Boolean “conjuntive queries” often result in either 
too few (=0) or too many (1000s) results.
▪Query 1: “standard user dlink 650” → 200,000 hits
▪Query 2: “standard user dlink 650 no card found”: 0 hits
▪It takes a lot of skill to come up with a query that 
produces a manageable number of hits.
▪AND gives too few; OR gives too many
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Ranked retrieval models
▪Rather than a set of documents satisfying a query 
expression, in ranked retrieval, the system reorders 
the (top) documents in the collection for a given 
query
▪Free text queries: Rather than a query language of 
operators and expressions, the user’s query is just 
one or more words in a natural language
▪the user specifies her/is information need through an ad-
hoc query which initiates a search
▪Ranked retrieval has normally been associated with 
free text queries and vice versa
7
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Feast or famine: not a problem in 
ranked retrieval
▪When a system produces a ranked result set, 
large result sets are not an issue
▪Indeed, the size of the result set is not an issue
▪We just show the top k ( ≈ 10) results
▪We don’t overwhelm the user
▪Premise: the ranking algorithm works ☺
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Scoring as the basis of ranked retrieval
▪We wish to return “in order” those documents 
▪First the ones most likely to be useful to users
▪How can we rank-order the documents in the 
collection with respect to a query?
▪Assign a score – say in [0, 1] – to each document 
given a query
▪This score measures how well document and query 
“match”
▪We need a way of assigning a score to a 
query/document pair
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring?
▪Recall: A commonly used measure of overlap of two 
sets A and B
▪jaccard(A,B) = |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|
▪jaccard(A,A) = 1
▪jaccard(A,B) = 0   if A ∩ B = 0
▪A and B don’t have to be of the same size.
▪Always assigns a number between 0 and 1
▪Higher is better
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring
▪What is the query-document Jaccard score for each 
of the two documents below?
▪Query: ides of march
▪Document 1: “Caesar died in March”
▪Document 2:  “The long march”
▪J(q, d1) = 1 / (3+4-1) = 1/6
▪J(q, d2) = 1 / (3+3-1) = 1/5
▪Using Jaccard d2 appears to be similar to q than d1 
▪However, the issue here is not Jaccard
Ch. 6
Assume lowercase 
term normalization
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Issues with Jaccard for scoring
▪It doesn't consider term frequency (how many times 
a term occurs in a document)
▪Jaccard doesn’t consider this information, as texts are 
represented as “sets” of terms, as in the Boolean model of 
IR 
▪Term frequency
▪The more frequent the query term in the document, the 
higher the score (should be)
▪Rare terms in a collection are more informative than 
frequent terms 
Ch. 6

text:
Bag of words model (multiset)
▪Count vector (bag of words) representation doesn't 
consider the ordering of words in a document
▪John is quicker than Mary and Mary is quicker than John 
have the same vectors
▪In a sense, this is a step back: The positional index was 
able to distinguish these two documents.
▪We will look at “recovering” positional information later in 
this course.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Notation
16
▪t  is a generic vocabulary term
▪cft is collection frequency: the number of occurrences 
of term t in the document collection 
▪dft is the document frequency: the total number of 
documents that contain term t in the corpus
▪tft,d is the term frequency: The total number of 
occurrences of term t in a document d
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Term frequency tf
▪The term frequency tft,d of term t in document d is 
defined as the count/number of times that t occurs in d
▪We want to use tft,d when computing query-document 
match scores. But how?
▪Raw term frequency is not what we want:
▪A document with 10 occurrences of the term is more relevant 
than a document with 1 occurrence of the term.
▪But not 10 times more relevant
▪Relevance does not increase linearly with term

text:
Document frequency
▪Rare terms in the collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
▪Small dft  w.r.t. the size N of the document corpus
▪dft ≪N
▪Consider a term in the query that is (globally) rare in 
the collection (e.g., arachnocentric)
▪A document containing this term is very likely to be relevant 
to the query arachnocentric
  
→ We want higher weights for rare terms like 
  
 
arachnocentric
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Document frequency, continued
▪Frequent terms in the collection are less informative 
than rare terms
▪Consider a query term that is frequent enough in the 
collection (e.g., high, increase, line)
▪A document containing such a term is more likely to be 
relevant than a document that doesn’t contain it
▪But it’s not a sure indicator of relevance wrt the user 
information need, as a large part of the documents 
contains this query term 
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Document frequency, continued
▪We will use document frequency (dft) to capture this
▪we want high positive weights for words like high, 
increase, and line (characterized by a large dft, but still 
much smaller than the collection size)
▪but lower than weights assigned to globally rare terms, 
characterized by a very small dft
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
How do we weight doc terms in the vectors?
▪Here’s the intuition:
▪Terms that appear often in a document (tf) should get high 
weights
▪Terms that appear in many documents (df) should get low 
weights
▪
How do we capture this mathematically?
▪Term frequency
▪Inverse document frequency
The more often a document contains the term “dog”, 
the more likely that the document is “about” dogs.
Words like “the”, “a”, “of” appear in (nearly) all 
documents.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
tf-idf weighting
▪The tf-idf weight of a term is the 
product of its tf weight (normalized wf) and its idf weight.
▪Best known weighting scheme in Information Retrieval
▪Note: the “-” in tf-idf is a hyphen, not a minus sign!
▪Alternative names:  tf.idf,   tf x idf
▪Increases with the number of occurrences within a document
▪Increases with the rarity of the term in the collection
Sec. 6.2.2

log(1 + tft,d)

text:
Documents as vectors
▪So, we have a |V|-dimensional vector space
▪Vocabulary’s terms are axes of the space
▪Documents are points or vectors in this space
▪Very high-dimensional: tens of millions of 
dimensions when you apply this to a web search 
engine
▪Very sparse vectors - most entries are zero.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Queries as vectors
▪Key idea 1: Do the same for queries: represent them 
as vectors in the space
▪Key idea 2: Rank documents according to their 
proximity to the query in this vectorial space
▪proximity = similarity of vectors
▪proximity ≈ inverse of distance
▪Recall: We do this because we want to get away from 
the either-in-or-out of the Boolean model.
▪Instead: rank more relevant documents higher than 
less relevant documents
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Formalizing vector space proximity
▪First cut: distance between two points
▪( = distance between the end points of the two vectors)
▪Euclidean distance?
▪Euclidean distance is a bad idea . . .
▪. . . because Euclidean distance is large for vectors of 
different lengths.
▪Euclidean distance works however for normalized 
vectors
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Use angle instead of distance
▪Experiment: take a document d and append it to 
itself. Call this document d′
▪d′ and d have the same semantic content
▪Vectors d′ and d overlap, but the magnitudes d′ is double
▪The Euclidean distance between the two documents 
d′ and d can be quite large
▪Although the angle between the two vectorial 
representations of the two documents is 0o (in the vector 
space)
▪Key idea: Rank document vectors according to the 
“angle” with the query vector.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
From angles to cosines
▪The following two notions are equivalent.
▪Rank documents in decreasing order of the angle between 
query and document      angle=distance
▪Rank documents in increasing order  of 
cosine(query,document) 
 cosine=similarity
▪Cosine is a monotonically decreasing function in the 
interval [0o, 180o]
▪from 1 (max similarity) to -1 (max dissimilarity)
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

text:
Length normalization
▪A vector can be (length-) normalized by dividing each 
of its components by its length – for this we use the L2 
norm:
▪Dividing a vector by its L2 norm makes it a unit (length) 
vector (on the surface of the unit hypersphere)
▪Effect on the two documents d and d′ (d appended to 
itself) from the earlier slide: they have identical vectors 
after length-normalization
▪Long and short documents have now comparable weights
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf (2).pdf

equivalently, the cosine of the angle between q and d.

text:

documents
▪Search engines adopt different weighting schemes for 
queries and documents
▪SMART Notation
▪the notation ddd.qqq (using the acronyms from the previous 
table) denotes the combination in use in a search engine
▪A very standard weighting scheme is: lnc.ltc
▪Document: logarithmic tf (l as 1st character), no idf (n as 2nd 
character), cosine normalization (c as 3rd character)
▪Query: logarithmic tf (l as 1st character),  idf (t as 2nd character), 
cosine normalization (c as 3rd character)
▪Indeed, raw tft in queries is usually 1 if a term occurs

text:
BM25
▪𝜏 is a saturation function, modeling non-linearity of 
term frequencies contribution
▪For high values of Ft, increments in term frequency don’t 
continue to contribute significantly to the score
▪b and k are hyper-parameters (should be tuned on the 
collection)
▪Typical values:  b =0.75 and k ∈[1.2, 2.0]

text:
Summary – vector space ranking
▪Represent the query as a weighted tf-idf vector
▪Represent each document as a weighted tf-idf vector
▪Compute the cosine similarity score for the query 
vector and each document vector
▪Rank documents with respect to the query by score
▪Return the top K (e.g., K = 10) to the user

text:
Ranked retrieval
▪So far, our queries have all been Boolean.
▪Documents either match or don’t.
▪Good for expert users with precise understanding of 
their needs and the collection.
▪Also good for applications: Applications can easily consume 
1000s of results.
▪Not good for most users.
▪Incapable of writing Boolean queries (or they are, but they 
think it’s too much work).
▪Don’t want to wade through 1000s of results.
▪This is particularly true of web search.
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Problem with Boolean search:
feast or famine
▪Boolean “conjuntive queries” often result in either 
too few (=0) or too many (1000s) results.
▪Query 1: “standard user dlink 650” → 200,000 hits
▪Query 2: “standard user dlink 650 no card found”: 0 hits
▪It takes a lot of skill to come up with a query that 
produces a manageable number of hits.
▪AND gives too few; OR gives too many
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Ranked retrieval models
▪Rather than a set of documents satisfying a query 
expression, in ranked retrieval, the system reorders 
the (top) documents in the collection for a given 
query
▪Free text queries: Rather than a query language of 
operators and expressions, the user’s query is just 
one or more words in a natural language
▪the user specifies her/is information need through an ad-
hoc query which initiates a search
▪Ranked retrieval has normally been associated with 
free text queries and vice versa
7
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Feast or famine: not a problem in 
ranked retrieval
▪When a system produces a ranked result set, 
large result sets are not an issue
▪Indeed, the size of the result set is not an issue
▪We just show the top k ( ≈ 10) results
▪We don’t overwhelm the user
▪Premise: the ranking algorithm works ☺
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Scoring as the basis of ranked retrieval
▪We wish to return “in order” those documents 
▪First the ones most likely to be useful to users
▪How can we rank-order the documents in the 
collection with respect to a query?
▪Assign a score – say in [0, 1] – to each document 
given a query
▪This score measures how well document and query 
“match”
▪We need a way of assigning a score to a 
query/document pair
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring?
▪Recall: A commonly used measure of overlap of two 
sets A and B
▪jaccard(A,B) = |A ∩B| / |A ∪B|
▪jaccard(A,A) = 1
▪jaccard(A,B) = 0 if A ∩ B = 0
▪A and B don’t have to be of the same size.
▪Always assigns a number between 0 and 1
▪Higher is better
Ch. 6
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Jaccard coefficient for scoring
▪What is the query-document Jaccard score for each 
of the two documents below?
▪Query: ides of march
▪Document 1: “Caesar died in March”
▪Document 2:  “The long march”
▪J(q, d1) = 1 / (3+4-1) = 1/6
▪J(q, d2) = 1 / (3+3-1) = 1/5
▪Using Jaccard d2 appears to be similar to q than d1 
▪However, the issue here is not Jaccard
Ch. 6
Assume lowercase 
term normalization
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Issues with Jaccard for scoring
▪It doesn't consider term frequency (how many times 
a term occurs in a document)
▪Jaccard doesn’t consider this information, as texts are 
represented as “sets” of terms, as in the Boolean model of 
IR 
▪Term frequency
▪The more frequent the query term in the document, the 
higher the score (should be)
▪Rare terms in a collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
Ch. 6

text:
Bag of words model (multiset)
▪Count vector (bag of words) representation doesn't 
consider the ordering of words in a document
▪John is quicker than Mary and Mary is quicker than John
have the same vectors
▪In a sense, this is a step back: The positional index was 
able to distinguish these two documents.
▪We will look at “recovering” positional information later in 
this course.

text:
Term frequency tf
▪The term frequency tft,d of term t in document d is 
defined as the count/number of times that t occurs in d
▪We want to use tft,d when computing query-document 
match scores. But how?
▪Raw term frequency is not what we want:
▪A document with 10 occurrences of the term is more relevant 
than a document with 1 occurrence of the term.
▪But not 10 times more relevant
▪Relevance does not increase linearly with term 
frequency

text:
Document frequency
▪Rare terms in the collection are more informative than 
frequent terms
▪Small dft w.r.t. the size N of the document corpus
▪dft ≪N
▪Consider a term in the query that is (globally) rare in 
the collection (e.g., arachnocentric)
▪A document containing this term is very likely to be relevant 
to the query arachnocentric
→ We want higher weights for rare terms like 
arachnocentric
Sec. 6.2.1
they are almost equal when working with stopwords.
so a very rare term

[NOTE] so a very rare term
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Document frequency, continued
▪Frequent terms in the collection are less informative 
than rare terms
▪Consider a query term that is frequent enough in the 
collection (e.g., high, increase, line)
▪A document containing such a term is more likely to be 
relevant than a document that doesn’t contain it
▪But it’s not a sure indicator of relevance wrt the user 
information need, as a large part of the documents
contains this query term
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Document frequency, continued
▪We will use document frequency (dft) to capture this
▪we want high positive weights for words like high, 
increase, and line (characterized by a large dft, but still 
much smaller than the collection size)
▪but lower than weights assigned to globally rare terms, 
characterized by a very small dft
Sec. 6.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
How do we weight doc terms in the vectors?
▪Here’s the intuition:
▪Terms that appear often in a document (tf) should get high 
weights
▪Terms that appear in many documents (df) should get low 
weights
▪
How do we capture this mathematically?
▪Term frequency
▪Inverse document frequency
The more often a document contains the term “dog”, 
the more likely that the document is “about” dogs.
Words like “the”, “a”, “of” appear in (nearly) all 
documents.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
tf-idf weighting
▪The tf-idf weight of a term is the 
product of its tf weight (normalized wf) and its idf weight.
▪Best known weighting scheme in Information Retrieval
▪Note: the “-” in tf-idf is a hyphen, not a minus sign!
▪Alternative names:  tf.idf,   tf x idf
▪Increases with the number of occurrences within a document
▪Increases with the rarity of the term in the collection
Sec. 6.2.2
Alternative smaller:
log(1 + tft,d)

text:
Documents as vectors
▪So, we have a |V|-dimensional vector space
▪Vocabulary’s terms are axes of the space
▪Documents are points or vectors in this space
▪Very high-dimensional: tens of millions of 
dimensions when you apply this to a web search 
engine
▪Very sparse vectors - most entries are zero.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Queries as vectors
▪Key idea 1: Do the same for queries: represent them 
as vectors in the space
▪Key idea 2: Rank documents according to their 
proximity to the query in this vectorial space
▪proximity = similarity of vectors
▪proximity ≈ inverse of distance
▪Recall: We do this because we want to get away from 
the either-in-or-out of the Boolean model.
▪Instead: rank more relevant documents higher than 
less relevant documents
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Formalizing vector space proximity
▪First cut: distance between two points
▪( = distance between the end points of the two vectors)
▪Euclidean distance?
▪Euclidean distance is a bad idea . . .
▪. . . because Euclidean distance is large for vectors of 
different lengths.
▪Euclidean distance works however for normalized 
vectors
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Why Euclidean distance is a bad idea
The Euclidean
distance between q
and d2 is large
although the
distribution of terms
in query q and 
document d2 are
very similar
Sec. 6.3
Euclidean distance
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Use angle instead of distance
▪Experiment: take a document d and append it to 
itself. Call this document d′
▪d′ and d have the same semantic content
▪Vectors d′ and d overlap, but the magnitudes d′ is double
▪The Euclidean distance between the two documents 
d′ and d can be quite large
▪Although the angle between the two vectorial 
representations of the two documents is 0o (in the vector 
space)
▪Key idea: Rank document vectors according to the 
“angle” with the query vector.
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
From angles to cosines
▪The following two notions are equivalent.
▪Rank documents in decreasing order of the angle between 
query and document     angle=distance
▪Rank documents in increasing order  of 
cosine(query,document) 
cosine=similarity
▪Cosine is a monotonically decreasing function in the 
interval [0o, 180o]
▪from 1 (max similarity) to -1 (max dissimilarity)
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

text:
Length normalization
▪A vector can be (length-) normalized by dividing each 
of its components by its length – for this we use the L2
norm:
▪Dividing a vector by its L2 norm makes it a unit (length) 
vector (on the surface of the unit hypersphere)
▪Effect on the two documents d and d′ (d appended to 
itself) from the earlier slide: they have identical vectors 
after length-normalization
▪Long and short documents have now comparable weights
Sec. 6.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture6-tfidf.pdf

cos(q,d) is the cosine similarity of q and d … or,
equivalently, the cosine of the angle between q and d.

text:
TAAT vs DAAT techniques
▪TAAT = “Term At A Time”
▪Doc scores docs are computed one “query term” at a time 
(we’ll also discuss Fast scoring to optimize TAAT)
▪We need to accumulate the per-term TF-IDF weights in per-doc 
accumulators 
▪DAAT = “Document At A Time”
▪Compute the total score for each doc (regarding all query 
terms) before proceeding to the next doc
▪TAAT requires 
▪a larger number of documents to be scored, and thus more 
memory to accumulate their scores before sorting
▪DAAT requires 
▪less memory, as less document scores should be stored overall 
(only the promising ones).

text:
Efficient cosine ranking
▪What we’re actually doing
▪solving the K-nearest neighbor problem for a query vector, 
where query and document vectors are high-dimensional 
and very sparse
▪It is solvable for short queries, as we deal with sparse 
query/document vectors and standard inverted files 
indexes support this well
▪In general, we don’t know how to do this so  
efficiently for high-dimensional spaces and dense 
vectors
▪We have however efficient approximate methos for kNN 
queries for low-dimensional and dense vectors (thousand 
of dimensions, a.k.a. document/query embeddings)

TAAT cons
▪A lot of cache misses due to many accumulators to 
be  incremented (jumps in memory)
▪No possibility of skipping docIDs for selective queries

▪Can we pick off docs with K highest cosines without totally 
ordering the associated scores?
▪Let J = number of docs with nonzero cosines, much less 
than N if query terms do not include stop-words
▪Indeed, we must detect the K best of these J scores 
(rather than N)

text:
Alternative: MaxHeap to select the top K
▪MaxHeap: Priority queue realized as 
▪a complete binary tree: if a node is stored at index k, then its 
left/right children are stored at index 2k+1 and 2k+2
▪node’s values are greater than (equal to) the values of children
▪Takes 2J comparisons to construct the MaxHeap, given 
the complete array of accumulators (similar to heap sort)
▪Then, each of K “winners” read off in log J steps.
▪Total operations: 2J+K log J
▪For J=1M, K=100, 
this is about 10% 
of the cost of sorting
the accumulators

text:
DAAT: Use MIN-heap of k elements 
▪Goal: Keep the top k documents seen so far
▪Use a binary min heap
▪To process a new document d′ with score s′:
▪Get current minimum hm of heap (O(1))
▪If s′ ˂= hm skip to next document
▪If s′ > hm heap-delete-root (O(log k))
▪Heap-add
s′:d′   (O(log k))
▪In the worst case:
▪we have to add to the min-heap all new docs

text:
DAAT algorithm for an n-term query
21
Input: A list of query terms Q = [t1, t2, ..., tn]
       A cutoff k (size of the sorted result set, top-k scored docIDs)
       For each term ti, a sorted posting list PLi of document docIDs with associated 
       weights. At a generic position n of each PLi, we have two components: 
       PLi[n].docID and PLi[n].weight.  
Output: A min_heap of size k, from which we can extract the sorted list of docIDs, 
where the sorting is done in reverse order of score
1. Initialize pointers pi = 0, for each posting list PLi
2. Initialize a min_heap of size k, threshold = 0 
3. while at least one pi is still < len(PLi):
     - current_docIDs = [PLi[pi].docID for each i in [1:n] if pi < len(Pli)]
     - min_docID = min(current_docIDSs)
     score_accumulator = 0.0
4.   for each i in [1:n] :
         if pi < len(PLi)] and PLi[pi].docID == min_docID:
             score_accumulator += PLi[pi].weight
             pi += 1 # advance the posting list pointer
5.   if len(min_heap) < k  or  score_accumulator > threshold:
         threshold = min_heap.add(min_docID, score_accumulator) # update min_heap
                                                      # and the associate threshold  
6. return min_heap

text:
WAND scoring
▪An instance of DAAT scoring
▪Normal DAAT still computes all the J scores
▪WAND: basic idea reminiscent of branch and bound
▪We maintain a running threshold score – e.g., the Kth 
highest score computed so far (the least score in the min-
heap)
▪We prune away all docs whose cosine scores are 
guaranteed to be below the current threshold
▪We compute exact cosine scores for only the un-pruned 
docs, much less than J scores

text:
WAND scoring
▪First level evaluation: quickly establish whether a 
document merits to be fully evaluated 
▪i.e., whether it has any chance of being a top-k candidate
▪No false negatives: cannot just throw away potential 
matches 
▪As few false positives, but fortunately, no full evaluation 
for all J documents
▪The second level is the full evaluation of a hopefully 
small number of candidates to enter the min-heap
23
Broder et al. Efﬁcient Query Evaluation using a Two-Level Retrieval Process.
Prune the docs that have no chance of being in the heap. Fals positive is fine.

text:
Index structure for WAND
▪Postings ordered by docID
▪Assume a special iterator on the postings of the form 
“go to the first docID equal to or greater than X”
▪Typical state: we have a “finger” at some docID in the 
postings of each query term
▪As usual, each finger moves only to the right, to larger 
docIDs
▪Invariant – all docIDs lower than any finger have 
already been processed, i.e.,
▪these docIDs have been already pruned away, or
▪the docIDs’ scores have been already computed

text:
WAND summary
▪In tests, WAND leads to a large reduction in score 
computation 
▪Better gains on longer queries and small cutoff k
▪Sort and pivoting with UBs is more expensive than DAAT, so 
speedup depends on the amount of pruned weights’ 
computations
▪Nothing we did is truly specific to cosine ranking
▪We only need scoring to be additive by term
▪WAND and variants give us a safe ranking (next slides)
▪Possible to devise “careless” variants that are a bit faster 
but not safe (see summary in Ding+Suel 2011)

text:
Block-Max
▪WAND stores the maximum “impact score” for each 
term/posting list
▪Max impact scores (UBt) in WAND can be much larger than the 
individual doc scores: many false positive
▪Block-Max: Augmented inverted index structure called a 
block-max index to make faster WAND
▪Block-Max stores the maximum “impact score” for each 
block of a compressed inverted list in uncompressed form
▪this enables the skip of large parts of the lists, without 
decompressing, with also less false positive than WAND
.
33
S. Ding and T. Suel. 2011. Faster top-k document retrieval using block-max indexes. SIGIR '11

▪Splits the inverted lists into blocks of, say, 64 or 128 
docIDs,  such that each block can be decompressed 
separately. 
▪Besides the global UBs per each posting list, exploit an 
extra table, which stores for each compressed block 
▪The maximum/minimum docID
▪Maximum impact value for each block (UBs per block)
▪The block size 
▪Reimplement a special iterator nextGEQ(p_list, docID) 
▪to avoid decompressing blocks (using the information about 
the block boundaries in the table) 
.

text:
UNSAFE RANKING
37
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Safe vs unsafe ranking
▪“Safe ranking” 
▪guarantee that the K docs returned are associated with the K 
highest scored documents, given a suitable similarity 
functions
▪TAAT, DAAT, WAND
▪“Unsafe ranking”  (Approximate ranking)
▪The result approximates the true top-K under the chosen 
similarity measure, possibly containing false positives
▪Avoiding computing similarity for all candidates
▪Is it acceptable to use an unsafe method?
▪Yes, ranking functions are a proxies for user happiness 
(document relevance)
38
So far, all methods returned the same top K
Does high cosine similarity really mean relevance?
In classical IR, two terms might be so far away that semantics are lost but the doc might still get a high score regardless.
ranking is perfectly with respect to score.
Normal DAAT does not have FP at all.
What if a doc scores high but is not relevant? The words in the query could be so far away.
Without positional index and some sort of similarity, this is no possible to check.
[NOTE] So far, all methods returned the same top K
[NOTE] Does high cosine similarity really mean relevance?
[NOTE] In classical IR, two terms might be so far away that semantics are lost but the doc might still get a high score regardless.
[NOTE] ranking is perfectly with respect to score.
[NOTE] Normal DAAT does not have FP at all.
[NOTE] What if a doc scores high but is not relevant? The words in the query could be so far away.
[NOTE] Without positional index and some sort of similarity, this is no possible to check.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
DAAT made unsafe
▪UNSAFE aggressive pruning: larger threshold τ’
▪the threshold can be artificially increased by using a new 
threshold τ′ = F ·τ, where F ≥ 1 is a tunable parameter. 
▪a document deserves to be fully evaluated only if the final score 
is likely much greater than the current threshold τ (i.e., the 
current min score of the heap)
▪more documents are pruned without computing the score
39
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Generic approach for UNSAFE ranking
▪Find a set A of contenders, with K <  |A|  << N
▪A will not necessarily contain the top K docs, but has 
many docs from among the real/safe top K 
▪Return the top K docs in A  (approximate top K)
▪Think of the creation of A as a process aiming to 
prune non-contenders
▪The same approach is also used for other (non-
cosine) scoring functions
▪Will look at several schemes following this approach
Sec. 7.1.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Index elimination
▪Basic algorithm for cosine computation only 
considers docs containing at least one query term
▪Take this further, considering that cosine 
computation is additive:
▪Only consider high-idf query terms (remove semi-
stopwords)
▪Only consider docs containing many query terms (soft 
AND)
Sec. 7.1.2
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
High-idf query terms only
▪Give a query such as    catcher in the rye
▪only accumulate scores from catcher and rye
▪Intuition: due to idf, the terms in and the contribute 
little to the additive scores and so don’t alter rank-
ordering too much
▪Benefit:
▪Postings of low-idf terms have many docs (long posting 
lists) 
▪These (many) docs get eliminated from set A of contenders 
unless they do not contain also catcher and rye
Sec. 7.1.2
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Docs containing many query terms
▪Any doc with at least one query term is a candidate for the 
top K output list
▪Cosine and other similarity measures are additive, so they get 
larger as we add the contributions of more terms
▪For multi-term queries, only compute scores for docs 
containing several of the query terms
▪Say, at least 3 out of 4
▪Imposes a “soft conjunction” on queries seen on web search engines 
(early Google)
▪Easy to implement in DAAT postings traversal
▪Still many docs get eliminated from set A of contenders, 
unless they do not contain many query terms
Sec. 7.1.2
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

Sec. 7.1.2
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Champion lists
▪Precompute for each dictionary term t, the r docs of 
highest weight in t’s postings
▪Call this the champion list for t
▪The champion lists are still ordered by DocID
▪At query time, only compute scores for docs in the 
champion list of some query term
▪Pick the K documents with the highest score from these
▪Note that r must be chosen at index building time
▪There is no reason to have the same value of r for all terms 
in the dictionary. For instance, for a rare term, we expect 
to include in the corresponding champion list most of its 
posting list 
Sec. 7.1.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Static quality scores
▪We want top-ranking documents to be both relevant 
and authoritative
▪Relevance is being modeled by cosine scores
▪Authority is typically a query-independent property 
of a document
▪Examples of authority signals
▪Wikipedia among websites
▪Articles in certain newspapers
▪A paper with many citations
▪PageRank
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Modeling authority
▪Assign to each document a query-independent 
quality score in [0,1] to each document d
▪Higher is better
▪Denote this by g(d)
▪Thus, a quantity like the number of citations must be 
normalized into [0,1]
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
g(d) = PageRank(d)
▪PageRank is a link analysis 
algorithm, named after Brin 
& Page [1], and used by the 
Google Internet search 
engine, which assigns a 
numerical weighting (a 
probability) to each element 
of a hyperlinked set of 
documents, such as the 
World Wide Web, with the 
purpose of "measuring" its 
relative importance within 
the set [wikipedia]
[1] Brin, S. and Page, L. (1998) The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual
Web Search Engine. In: Seventh International World-Wide Web Conference 
(WWW 1998), April 14-18, 1998, Brisbane, Australia
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Net score
▪Consider a simple total score combining cosine 
relevance and authority
▪net-score(q,d) = g(d) + cosine(q,d)
▪Can use some other linear (convex) combination
▪Indeed, any function of the two “signals” of user happiness 
– more later
▪Now we seek the top K docs by net score
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Top K by net score – fast methods
▪First idea: Order all postings by g(d)
▪Key: this is a common ordering for all postings, and is 
obtained by remapping the docID
▪DocID sorting is kept
▪the smallest docID for the largest g(d)
▪the largest docID for the smallest g(d) 
▪Thus, can concurrently traverse query terms’ 
postings by exploiting DocID sorting 
▪Postings intersection (conjunctive, docs containing many 
query terms)
▪DAAT, WAND, etc.
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Why order postings by g(d)?
▪Under g(d)-ordering
▪top-scoring docs (if we only consider the component g(d) 
of the scoring function) appear early in postings traversal
▪In time-bound applications
▪say, we must return whatever search results we can in 50 
ms (time budget)
▪g(d)-ordering allows us to stop postings traversal early 
(early termination) with good results
▪Avoiding computing scores for all docs in postings
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Champion lists in g(d)-ordering
▪Can combine champion lists with g(d)-ordering
▪Maintain for each term a champion list of the r docs 
with highest g(d) + tf-idftd
▪Seek top-K results from only the docs in these 
champion lists
▪Do you see any issue with this scheme? 
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
High and low lists
▪For each term, we maintain two postings lists called 
high and low
▪Think of high as the champion list
▪When traversing postings on a query, only traverse 
high lists first
▪If we get more than K docs, select the top K and stop
▪Else proceed to get docs from the low lists
▪Can be used even for simple cosine scores, without 
global quality g(d)
▪A means for segmenting index into two tiers
Sec. 7.1.4
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Tiered indexes
▪Break postings up into a hierarchy of lists
▪Most important
▪…
▪Least important
▪Can be done by g(d) or another measure, like 
g(d) + tf-idftd
▪The inverted index is thus broken up into tiers of 
decreasing importance
▪At query time, only use the top tier, unless it fails to 
yield K docs
▪If so, drop to the lower tiers
Sec. 7.2.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Tiered Index
•
A sequence of sub-indexes
- former sub-indexes are small 
and keep more important 
documents
- later sub-indexes are larger and 
keep less important documents
- a query is processed selectively 
only on the first n tiers
•
Two decisions need to be made
- tiering (offline): how to place 
documents in different tiers
- fall-through (online): at which 
tier to stop processing the query
3rd tier
index
1st tier

text:
Example tiered index
Sec. 7.2.1
•
For each term, order the postings by g(d) + tf-idftd
•
Then distribute to the tiers accordingly, considering the impact
•
Note that the same doc may appear in different tiers
The per-tier posting 
lists must be 
reordered by DocID

Document Clustering and Index Pruning 
via Collection Selection
• Document clustering using 
different similarity metrics
- similarity between documents
- co-click likelihood
- A separate smaller index is built for each
document cluster / collection
- Collection selection

representative doc/docs

text:
Impact-ordered postings
▪We only want to compute scores for docs for which 
wft,d is high enough
▪We sort each postings list by wft,d
▪Now: list are no more sorted by DocID!
▪Only TAAT processing is possible
▪DAAT would “search” all the contributions for a given 
DocID to compute the final score
▪How do we compute scores in order to pick off top K, 
without computing all the scores (UNSAFE)?
▪Two ideas follow
Sec. 7.1.5
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
1. Early termination
▪When traversing t’s postings, stop early after either
▪a fixed number of r docs, or
▪wft,d  drops below some threshold
▪Take the union of the resulting sets of docs
▪A subset from the postings of each query term contributes 
to the union
▪Compute only the scores for docs in this union
Sec. 7.1.5
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
2. idf-ordered terms
▪When considering the postings of query terms, look 
at them in order of decreasing idf
▪High idf terms likely to contribute most to score
▪As we update the score contribution from each 
query term
▪stop if doc scores are relatively unchanged
▪This can apply to cosine or some other net scores
Sec. 7.1.5
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

Putting it all together

Linguistic processing + Indexing
Copy each parsed document in a document cache, for 
generating document snippets in the SERP

text:
Putting it all together
Sec. 7.2.4
Bank of indexers that create a bank of indexes
•
zone and field indexes (metadata), (tiered) positional indexes, 
indexes for spelling correction and other tolerant retrieval, and 
structures for accelerating inexact top-K retrieval.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Parametric/Field and zone indexes
▪Thus far, a doc has been a sequence of terms
▪Indeed, documents have multiple parts (semi-
structured), and some metadata associated:
▪Author
▪Title
▪Date of publication
▪Language
▪Format
▪etc.
Sec. 6.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Parameters/Fields
▪We sometimes wish to search using these metadata
▪E.g., find docs authored by William Shakespeare in the 
year 1601, containing alas poor Yorick
▪Year = 1601 is an example of a field
▪Also, author's last name = Shakespeare, etc.
▪Parametric or Field index: postings for each field 
value
▪Sometimes build range trees (e.g., for dates)
▪Field query is typically treated as a conjunction
▪(doc must be authored by Shakespeare)
Sec. 6.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Zone
▪A zone is an unstructured region of the doc that can 
contain an arbitrary amount of text, e.g.,
▪Title
▪Abstract
▪References …
▪Build inverted indexes on zones as well to permit 
querying
▪E.g., “find docs with merchant in the title zone and 
matching the query gentle rain”
Sec. 6.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Example zone indexes
Encode zones in dictionary vs. postings.
Sec. 6.1

text:
Term proximity (Positional indexes) & 
Vector space model
▪Free text queries: just a set of terms typed into the query box – 
common on the web
▪Users prefer docs in which query terms occur within “close 
proximity” of each other
▪Let ω be the smallest window in a doc containing all query terms, e.g.,
▪For the query strained mercy the smallest window in the doc The 
quality of mercy is not strained is 4 (words)
Sec. 7.2.2
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Aggregate scores
▪We would like “scoring function” to take proximity 
into account
▪ω as yet another feature in the scoring function, besides 
g(d) and cosine/BM25
▪How do we know the best combination?
▪Some applications – expert-tuned
▪Increasingly common: machine-learned
▪Learning to rank
Sec. 7.2.3
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace.pdf

text:
Putting it all together

Free text user query sent down to the indexes both
directly and through a module for spelling-correction
•
the latter may optionally be invoked only when the original
query fails to retrieve enough results.

weight and its idf weight.
▪Best known weighting scheme in information retrieval
▪Increases with the number of occurrences within a 
document
▪Increases with the rarity of the term in the collection

text:
Recap: Queries as vectors
▪Key idea 1: Do the same for queries: represent them 
as vectors in the space
▪Key idea 2: Rank documents according to their 
proximity to the query in this space
▪proximity = similarity of vectors
Ch. 6

text:
Recap: cosine(query,document)
cos(q,d) is the cosine similarity of q and d … or,
equivalently, the cosine of the angle between q and d.

text:

text:
TAAT vs DAAT techniques
▪TAAT = “Term At A Time”
▪Doc scores docs are computed one “query term” at a time 
(we’ll also discuss Fast scoring to optimize TAAT)
▪We need to accumulate the per-term TF-IDF weights in per-doc 
accumulators 
▪DAAT = “Document At A Time”
▪Compute the total score for each doc (regarding all query 
terms) before proceeding to the next doc
▪TAAT requires 
▪a larger number of documents to be scored, and thus more 
memory to accumulate their scores before sorting
▪DAAT requires 
▪less memory, as less document scores should be stored overall 
(only the promising ones).

text:
Efficient cosine ranking
▪Find the K docs in the collection “nearest” to the 
query  K largest query-doc cosines.
▪Efficient ranking:
▪Computing the single cosine values efficiently
▪Choosing the K largest cosine values efficiently.
▪Can we do this without computing all N cosines?
Sec. 7.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Efficient cosine ranking
▪What we’re actually doing
▪solving the K-nearest neighbor problem for a query vector, 
where query and document vectors are high-dimensional 
and very sparse
▪It is solvable for short queries, as we deal with sparse 
query/document vectors and standard inverted files 
indexes support this well
▪In general, we don’t know how to do this so  
efficiently for high-dimensional spaces and dense 
vectors
▪We have however efficient approximate methos for kNN 
queries for low-dimensional and dense vectors (thousand 
of dimensions, a.k.a. document/query embeddings) 
Sec. 7.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

Fast scoring – unweighted queries
▪No weighting on query terms
▪Assume each query term occurs only once (tft,q=1)
▪Indeed, we have   log (1 + tft,q) = 1 for  tft,q =1
▪idf is only used for computing wft,d and not for 
wft,q 
▪No length normalization of the query vector
▪Slight simplification of the scoring algorithm
▪only additive contribution

text:
TAAT cons
▪A lot of cache misses due to many accumulators to 
be  incremented (jumps in memory)
▪No possibility of skipping docIDs for selective queries 
(e.g., AND)
▪Need to sort huge numbers of accumulators
▪Can we pick off docs with K highest cosines without totally 
ordering the associated scores?
▪Let J = number of docs with nonzero cosines, much less 
than N if query terms do not include stop-words
▪Indeed, we must detect the K best of these J scores 
(rather than N)
Sec. 7.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Alternative: MaxHeap to select the top K
▪MaxHeap: Priority queue realized as 
▪a complete binary tree: if a node is stored at index k, then its 
left/right children are stored at index 2k+1 and 2k+2
▪node’s values are greater than (equal to) the values of children
▪Takes 2J comparisons to construct the MaxHeap, given 
the complete array of accumulators (similar to heap sort)
▪Then, each of K “winners” read off in log J steps.
▪Total operations: 2J+K log J
▪For J=1M, K=100, 
this is about 10% 
of the cost of sorting
the accumulators

DAAT: Use MIN-heap of k elements 
▪Use a binary min heap of k elements
▪Takes O(log k) 
operations insert a
new accumulator 
in the MIN-heap . . .
▪. . . then read off k 
winners in order

source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
DAAT: Use MIN-heap of k elements 
▪Goal: Keep the top k documents seen so far
▪Use a binary min heap
▪To process a new document d′ with score s′:
▪Get current minimum hm of the heap (O(1))
▪If s′ ˂= hm skip to next document
▪If s′ > hm heap-delete-root (O(log k))
▪Heap-add
s′:d′   (O(log k))
▪In the worst case:
▪we have to add to the min-heap all new docs
19

text:
DAAT algorithm for an n-term query
21
Input: A list of query terms Q = [t1, t2, ..., tn]
       A cutoff k (size of the sorted result set, top-k scored docIDs)
       For each term ti, a sorted posting list PLi of document docIDs with associated 
       weights. At a generic position n of each PLi, we have two components: 
       PLi[n].docID and PLi[n].weight.  
Output: A min_heap of size k, from which we can extract the sorted list of docIDs, 
where the sorting is done in reverse order of score
1. Initialize pointers pi = 0, for each posting list PLi
2. Initialize a min_heap of size k, threshold = 0 
3. while at least one pi is still < len(PLi):
     - current_docIDs = [PLi[pi].docID for each i in [1:n] if pi < len(Pli)]
     - min_docID = min(current_docIDSs)
     score_accumulator = 0.0
4.   for each i in [1:n] :
         if pi < len(PLi)] and PLi[pi].docID == min_docID:
             score_accumulator += PLi[pi].weight
             pi += 1 # advance the posting list pointer
5.   if len(min_heap) < k  or  score_accumulator > threshold:
         threshold = min_heap.add(min_docID, score_accumulator) # update min_heap
                                                      # and the associate threshold  
6. return min_heap
Like an OR query (disjunctive query)

text:
WAND scoring
▪An instance of DAAT scoring
▪Normal DAAT still computes all the J scores
▪WAND: basic idea reminiscent of branch and bound
▪We maintain a running threshold score – e.g., the Kth 
highest score computed so far (the least score in the min-
heap)
▪We prune away all docs whose cosine scores are 
guaranteed to be below the current threshold
▪We compute exact cosine scores for only the un-pruned 
docs, much less than J scores
22
Broder et al. Efﬁcient Query Evaluation using a Two-Level Retrieval Process.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
WAND scoring
▪First level evaluation: quickly establish whether a 
document merits to be fully evaluated 
▪i.e., whether it has any chance of being a top-k candidate
▪No false negatives: cannot just throw away potential 
matches 
▪As few false positives, but fortunately, no full evaluation 
for all J documents
▪The second level is the full evaluation of a hopefully 
small number of candidates to enter the min-heap
23
Broder et al. Efﬁcient Query Evaluation using a Two-Level Retrieval Process.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Index structure for WAND
▪Postings ordered by docID
▪Assume a special iterator on the postings of the form 
“go to the first docID equal to or greater than X”
▪Typical state: we have a “finger” at some docID in the 
postings of each query term
▪As usual, each finger moves only to the right, to larger 
docIDs
▪Invariant – all docIDs lower than any finger have 
already been processed, i.e.,
▪these docIDs have been already pruned away, or
▪the docIDs’ scores have been already computed
24
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Upper bounds and Thresholds
▪Upper bound for the posting list of term t
▪for each posting list associated with a query term t, we maintain 
an upper bound on the score contribution of any doc in the list
▪UBt :  idft * M,  where M = max wt,d for all d in the 
posting list of t
▪Threshold (τ)
▪Set Threshold to the min score of the heap 
▪Whenever a document d succeeds in entering the min-heap,

text:
WAND summary
▪In tests, WAND leads to a large reduction in score 
computation 
▪Better gains on longer queries and small cutoff k
▪Sort and pivoting with UBs is more expensive than DAAT, so 
speedup depends on the amount of pruned weights’ 
computations
▪Nothing we did is truly specific to cosine ranking
▪We only need scoring to be additive by term
▪WAND and variants give us a safe ranking (next slides)
▪Possible to devise “careless” variants that are a bit faster 
but not safe (see summary in Ding+Suel 2011)
32
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Block-Max
▪WAND stores the maximum “impact score” for each 
term/posting list
▪Max impact scores (UBt) in WAND can be much larger than the 
individual doc scores: many false positive
▪Block-Max: Augmented inverted index structure called a 
block-max index to make faster WAND
▪Block-Max stores the maximum “impact score” for each 
block of a compressed inverted list in uncompressed form
▪this enables the skip of large parts of the lists, without 
decompressing, with also less false positive than WAND
.
33
S. Ding and T. Suel. 2011. Faster top-k document retrieval using block-max indexes. SIGIR '11
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Block-Max
▪Splits the inverted lists into blocks of, say, 64 or 128 
docIDs,  such that each block can be decompressed 
separately. 
▪Besides the global UBs per each posting list, exploit an 
extra table, which stores for each compressed block 
▪The maximum/minimum docID
▪Maximum impact value for each block (UBs per block)
▪The block size 
▪Reimplement a special iterator nextGEQ(p_list, docID) 
▪to avoid decompressing blocks (using the information about 
the block boundaries in the table) 
.

Block-Max

▪maximum scores in each block
▪Observe the bottom “kangaroo” list, which shows a block 
with various impact scores

text:
UNSAFE RANKING
37
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Safe vs unsafe ranking
▪“Safe ranking” 
▪guarantee that the K docs returned are associated with the K 
highest scored documents, given a suitable similarity 
functions
▪TAAT, DAAT, WAND
▪“Unsafe ranking”  (Approximate ranking)
▪The result approximates the true top-K under the chosen 
similarity measure, possibly containing false positives
▪Avoiding computing similarity for all candidates
▪Is it acceptable to use an unsafe method?
▪Yes, ranking functions are a proxies for user happiness 
(document relevance)
38
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
Generic approach for UNSAFE ranking
▪Find a set A of contenders, with K <  |A|  << N
▪A will not necessarily include the top K docs of SAFE 
ranking, but has many docs from among the real/safe 
top K 
▪Return the top K docs in A  (approximate top K)
▪Think of the creation of A as a process aiming to 
prune non-contenders
▪The same approach is also used for other (non-
cosine) scoring functions
▪Will look at several schemes following this approach
Sec. 7.1.1
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture7-vectorspace2.pdf

text:
WAND made unsafe
▪WAND is already able to strongly reduce the documents 
that are evaluated to enter the top-k ranking
▪WAND is however SAFE
▪WAND can be made UNSAFE by exploting a more 
aggressive pruning: larger threshold τ’
▪the threshold can be artificially increased by using a new 
threshold τ′ = F ·τ, where F ≥ 1 is a tunable parameter. 
▪a document deserves to be fully evaluated only if the final 
score is likely much greater than the current threshold τ (i.e., 
the current min score of the heap)
▪more documents are pruned/skipped without computing the 
score, thus reducing the size of the set A of contenders 
40
The top of min heap. When a doc is greater 
than t` it can enter so we become stricter. Only 
scores much larger than min can enter.
We prune much more docs and reduce the set of contenders, reducing the number of scores to be computed. Fast but less precise.

text:
Index elimination
▪Basic algorithm for cosine computation only 
considers docs containing at least one query term
▪Take this further, considering that cosine 
computation is additive:
▪Only consider high-idf query terms (remove the docs in 
the posting lists of stopwords and semi-stopwords)
▪low-idf terms contribute little to the additive cosine score

text:
High-idf query terms only
▪Give a query such as    catcher in the rye
▪only accumulate scores from catcher and rye
▪Intuition: due to idf, the terms in and the contribute 
little to the additive scores and so don’t alter rank-
ordering too much
▪Benefit:
▪Postings of low-idf terms have many docs (long posting 
lists) 
▪These (many) docs get eliminated from set A of contenders 
unless they do not contain also catcher and rye

text:
Docs containing many query terms
▪Any doc with at least one query term is a candidate for the 
top K output list
▪Cosine and other similarity measures are additive, so they get 
larger as we add the contributions of more terms
▪For multi-term queries, only compute scores for docs 
containing several of the query terms
▪Say, at least 3 out of 4
▪Imposes a “soft AND” on queries seen on web search engines (early 
Google)
▪Easy to implement in DAAT postings traversal
▪Still many docs get eliminated from set A of contenders, 
unless they do not contain many query terms

text:
Champion lists
▪Precompute for each dictionary term t, the r docs of 
highest weight in t’s postings
▪Call this the champion list for t
▪The champion lists are still ordered by DocID
▪At query time, only compute scores for docs included 
in champion lists of query terms
▪Still, many docs are eliminated from set A of contenders, 
from with we pick the K documents with the highest scores
▪Note that r must be chosen at index building time
▪There is no reason to have the same value of r for all terms 
in the dictionary. For instance, for a rare term (high idf), we 
expect to include in the corresponding champion list most of 
its posting list 
Sec. 7.1.3

text:
Why order postings by g(d)?
▪Under g(d)-ordering
▪top-scoring docs (if we only consider the component g(d) 
of the scoring function) appear early in postings traversal
▪In time-bound applications
▪say, we must return whatever search results we can in 50 
ms (time budget)
▪g(d)-ordering allows us to stop postings traversal early 
(early termination) with good results
▪Avoiding computing scores for all docs in postings
▪Still, many docs get eliminated from set A of contenders

▪For each term, we maintain two postings lists called

of contenders

▪Can be used even for simple cosine scores, without 
global quality g(d)

text:
Tiered indexes
▪Break postings up into a hierarchy of lists
▪Most important
▪…
▪Least important
▪Can be done by g(d) or another measure, like 
g(d) + tf-idftd
▪The inverted index is thus broken up into tiers of 
decreasing importance
▪At query time, only use the top tier, unless it fails to 
yield K docs
▪If so, drop to the lower tiers
Sec. 7.2.1

text:
Tiered Index
•
A sequence of sub-indexes
- former sub-indexes are small 
and keep more important 
documents (e.g. docs with the 
highest weight g(d) + tf-idftd)
- later sub-indexes are larger and 
keep less important documents
- a query is processed selectively 
only on a subset of tiers
•
Two decisions need to be made
- tiering (offline): how to place 
documents in different tiers
- fall-through (online): at which 
tier to stop processing the query

text:
TREC
Sec. 8.5
▪Text REtrieval Conference (TREC):
▪Aims: creating an evaluation infrastructure required for large-
scale testing of information retrieval (IR) technology. 
▪This includes research on best methods for evaluation as well 
as development of the evaluation materials themselves. 
▪“Retrieval technology” is broadly interpreted to include a 
variety of techniques that enable and/or facilitate access to 
information not specifically structured for machine use. 
▪Sponsored by Nat. Inst. of Standards and Technology (NIST) 
▪TREC is organized around a set of focus areas called “tracks”. 
A track has a motivating use case, which is generally an 
abstraction of a user task 
(https://trec.nist.gov/pubs/trec32/xref.html)
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture8-evaluation.pdf

text:
TREC
Sec. 8.5
▪Test collections consist of three parts: 
1.
a set of documents
2.
a set of information needs (called topics in TREC)
3.
relevance judgments, an indication of which documents 
should be retrieved in response to which topics. 
▪We call the result of a retrieval system executing a 
task/query on a test collection a run.
A run is a subset of docs related to a query.
[NOTE] A run is a subset of docs related to a query.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture8-evaluation.pdf

text:
Document sampling and relevance 
judgments
▪“Pooling” is the technique used in early TRECs for 
selecting the sample of documents for the human 
assessor to judge. 
▪In pooling, the top results from a set of runs are combined 
to form the pool and only those documents in the pool are 
judged. 
▪Runs are subsequently evaluated assuming that all 
unpooled (and hence unjudged) documents are not 
relevant 
▪Several TREC tracks have investigated new ways of 
sampling from very large documents sets to obtain 
judgment sets that support fair evaluations.

text:
F-Measure or F-Score
▪F-Measure is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall:
▪The harmonic mean is always less than or equal to the 
arithmetic mean and the geometric mean. 
▪When the values of two numbers differ greatly, the harmonic 
mean is closer to their minimum than to their arithmetic mean
▪Consider an example with recall = 100% and precision very low:

text:
Mean average precision (MAP)
▪MAP is macro-averaging: each query counts equally
▪MAP is the arithmetic mean of the AP values
▪it weighs each query (information need) equally, even if 
many documents are relevant to some queries, whereas 
very few to others
▪Now perhaps one of the most used measures in 
research papers for binary relevance
▪Specifically, in search engine (and recommender 
systems) we adopt MAP@k 
▪K is an arbitrary cut-off rank chosen to limit evaluation
▪Adopted as we expect the user to engage only with a 
limited number of items shown to them
Query 2 is more difficult as there are only 3 relevant docs to begin with, MAP does not take this into account.
typically, k =10 as we expect the user to interact with a limited 
number of results.

text:
Precision@k
▪Averaged 11-point recall-precision curve and simple 
MAP are not good for web search
▪They factor in precision at all recall levels
▪In Web Search the total number of relevant docs is 
unknown, or these docs could be huge
▪What matters in Web Search is rather how many 
good results there are on the first page pages (e.g., 
one/three pages, 10/30 results)
▪P@K (Precision at K)
▪K is the cut-off rank
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture8-evaluation.pdf

text:
Precision@K
▪Set a rank threshold K
▪Compute % relevant in top K
▪Ignores documents ranked lower than K
▪P@K = ∑ relevant docs in top-K  /  K 
▪Ex:                  
▪Prec@3 of 2/3 = 0.66 
▪Prec@4 of 2/4 = 0.5
▪Prec@5 of 3/5 = 0.4
▪In a similar fashion, Recall@K
Note that Prec@4 is 
smaller than Prec@3

text:
Discounted Cumulative Gain
▪Popular measure for evaluating web search and 
related tasks
▪Two assumptions:
▪Highly relevant documents are more useful 
than marginally relevant documents
▪The lower the ranked position of a relevant 
document, the less useful it is for the user, 
since it is less likely to be examined

text:
Discounted Cumulative Gain
▪Uses graded relevance as a measure of  
usefulness, or gain, from examining a document
▪Gain is accumulated starting at the top of the 
ranking and may be reduced, or discounted, at 
lower ranks
▪Typical discount is 1/ log(rank)
▪With base 2, the discount at rank 4 is 1/2, and 
at rank 8 it is 1/3

▪Relevance judgments on a scale of [0,m],   m>2
▪Cumulative Gain (CG) at rank n
▪Let the judgements of the n documents be 
      r1, r2, …rn (in ranked order)
▪CG = r1+ r2+…rn
▪Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) at rank n
▪The gain is discounted if the relevant docs 
appear at a ranks > 1
▪DCG = r1 + r2/log22 + r3/log23 + … rn/log2n
▪We may use any base for the logarithm
