text:
Summarize a Ranking: NDCG
▪Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) 
at rank n
▪Normalize DCG at rank n by the DCG value at rank n of 
the ideal ranking
▪The ideal ranking would first return the documents with 
the highest relevance level, then the next highest 
relevance level, etc.
▪There are ties …
▪Normalization useful for contrasting queries with 
varying numbers of relevant results
▪NDCG is now quite popular in evaluating Web 
search

text:
Comparing a ranking to a baseline one 
with Kendal tau correlation
▪Given a set of pairwise preferences P (ground truth)
▪w/o relevance judgments assigned to documents 
▪We want to measure two rankings A and B

text:
Kendall tau correlation 
     (with total ordering without ties)
▪Let X be the number of agreements between a ranking (say A) 
and P, and Y be the number of disagreements
▪Then the Kendall tau between A and P is: τ =(X-Y)/(X+Y)
▪Example:
▪P = (a, b, c, d)
▪in terms of pairs:    {(a,b), (a,c), (a,d), (b,c), (b,d), (c,d)} 
▪A= (a, c, b, d)     
▪in terms of pairs:    {(a,c), (a,b), (a,d), (c,b), (c,d), (b,d)} 
▪Thus (b,c) doesn’t hold
▪Then X=5, Y=1 …   τ = 4/6 = 0.67
▪−1 ≤τ ≤1
▪1 (perfect agreement), -1 (perfect disagreement, i.e., one ranking is 
the reverse of the other), 0 (X and Y are independent)
▪There exists formulations with ties and partial ordering

text:
Relevance
▪We evaluate the quality of an information retrieval 
system. Specifically, its ranking algorithm with 
respect to relevance
▪A document is relevant if it gives the user the information 
she was looking for. 
▪To evaluate relevance, we need an evaluation 
benchmark with three elements: 
▪A benchmark document collection
▪A benchmark suite of queries
▪An assessment of the relevance of each query-document 
pair 
2
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture9-expansion.pdf

text:
Relevance: query vs. information need 
▪Information need: You are looking for information on whether 
drinking red wine is more effective at reducing your risk of 
heart attacks than white wine. 
▪Query q: wine AND red AND white AND heart AND attack 
▪Consider document d′: He then launched into the heart of his 
speech and attacked the wine industry lobby for downplaying 
the role of red and white wine in drunk driving. 
▪d′ is relevant to the query q, 
but d′ is not relevant to the information need
▪User happiness/satisfaction (i.e., how well our ranking 
algorithm works) can only be measured by relevance to 
information needs, not by relevance to queries.

text:
Vocabulary mismatch problem 
in classical IR
▪Consider query q: [aircraft] . . . 
▪. . . and document d containing “plane”, but not 
containing  “aircraft” 
▪A simple IR system will not return d for q. 
▪Even if d is the most relevant document for q !! 
▪Relevance feedback & query expansion
▪both improve recall and aim to return relevant 
documents even if there is no term matches for 
the (original) query

Best known method: Rocchio feedback 
▪Query expansion: improve retrieval results by adding

text:
Relevance feedback: basic idea
1. The user issues a (short, simple) query. 
2. The search engine returns a set of documents. 
3. User marks some docs as relevant, some as non-
relevant. 
4. The search engine computes a new query to 
represent the user information need. Hope: better 
than the initial query. 
5. Search engine runs new query and returns new 
results. New results have (hopefully) better recall.

text:
Rocchio algorithm
13
▪The Rocchio algorithm implements relevance feedback in the vector 
space model. 
▪Rocchio aims to choose an (artificial) query maximizing:
Dr : set of known relevant docs;   Dnr : set of known nonrelevant docs 
▪Maximize similarity with relevant documents, while minimizing similarity with 
non-relevant documents. 
▪Under cosine similarity,  if we knew Cr and Cnr , i.e., the set of all the 
relevant/not relevant documents
▪                should be the vector difference between the two centroids

text:
Rocchio algorithm
14
▪The optimal query vector is the vector difference between the two 
vectors associated with the two centroids, one for all the relevant 
documents and one for 
the not relevant ones
▪This observation is not terribly useful, as Cr (the full set of relevant 
documents) is unknown:  it is what we want to find.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lecture9-expansion.pdf

text:
Rocchio 1971 Algorithm (SMART)
▪Used in practice:
▪Dr  = set of known relevant doc vectors
▪Dnr = set of known irrelevant doc vectors
▪Different from Cr and Cnr
▪qm = modified query vector; 
▪q0 = original query vector; 
▪α,β,γ: weights (hand-chosen or set empirically)
▪New query moves toward relevant documents and away 
from irrelevant documents

text:
Relevance feedback: problems
18
▪Relevance feedback is expensive. 
▪Relevance feedback creates long modified queries, as the 
resulting vectors are less sparse 
▪Long queries are expensive to process!
▪Users are reluctant to provide explicit feedback. 
▪It’s often hard to understand why a particular document 
was retrieved after applying relevance feedback. 
▪The search engine Excite had full relevance feedback at 
one point but abandoned it later. 
More posting lists to go through

text:
Pseudo-relevance feedback
19
▪Pseudo-relevance feedback automates the “manual” 
part of true relevance feedback.
▪Pseudo-relevance feedback algorithm: 
▪Retrieve a ranked list of hits for the user’s query 
▪Assume that the top k documents are relevant. 
▪Apply the relevance feedback method to modify the query 
(e.g., Rocchio) 
▪It works very well on average but can go horribly wrong 
for some queries. 
▪Because of query drift
▪If you do several iterations of pseudo-relevance feedback, then 
you will get query drift for a large proportion of queries. 
We want to increase the recall.

text:
Thesaurus-based query expansion 
24
▪For each term t in the query, expand the query with words the 
thesaurus lists as semantically related to t 
▪Example:   hospital → medical 
▪Generally, increases recall 
▪May significantly decrease precision, particularly with ambiguous

▪interest rate → interest rate fascination 
▪Widely used in specialized search engines for science and 
engineering 
▪It’s very expensive to create a manual thesaurus and maintain it 
over time

text:
Automatic thesaurus generation
26
▪Attempt to generate a thesaurus automatically, 
▪Fundamental notion: similarity between two words 
▪Definition 1: Two words are similar if they co-occur with similar 
words
▪“car” ≈ “motorcycle” because both occur with “road”, “gas” and “license”, 
so they must be similar. 
▪This is also the basis of some deep learning findings: word embedding
▪Definition 2: Two words are similar if they occur in a given 
grammatical relation with the same words. 
▪You can harvest, peel, eat, prepare, etc. “apples” and “pears”, so “apples” 
and “pears” must be similar. 
▪NLP techniques to derive a grammatical relation (in this case, 
verb->object)

text:
Representing words by their context
29
• Distributional semantics: A word’s meaning is given by the 
words that frequently appear close-by (i.e., the word context)
• One of the most successful ideas of modern statistical NLP! 
• When the word w appears in a text, its context is the set of 
words that appear nearby (within a fixed-size window)
• We use the many contexts of w to build up a representation of w
 .. Aston Martin - the luxury British sport car manufacturer. Find your local dealer…
.. each and every Alfa Romeo car and SUV at Alfa Romeo …
.. full electric car of FIAT ..
   The surrounding context words represent car

text:
Word embeddings
30
• Building dense vector for each word w, chosen so that it is 
similar to vectors of words that appear in similar context
• We can measure similarity as the vector dot (scalar) product
• Word vectors are also called (word) embeddings or (neural) 
word representations
• They are a distributed representation
• Word2vec (Mikolov et al. 2013) – more details in following 
lectures

text:
Learning to Rank approaches
▪Pointwise
▪Each query-document pair is associated with a score
▪The objective is to predict such score
▪can be considered a pure regression problem or a multiclass classification problem
▪Does not consider the position of a document into the result list
▪Pairwise
▪Based on pairwise preferences: d1 is better than d2 for query q
▪The objective is to predict a score that preserves such preferences
▪Can also be considered a binary classification problem
▪It doesn’t consider graded relevance assigned to documents into the result 
list
▪Listwise
▪We are given the ideal ranking of results for each query
▪Objective is to maximize the quality of the whole resulting ranked list by 
exploiting somehow the whole list at training time 
▪We need some improved approach … to maximize NDCG@K
▪Usually we cannot directly apply (Stochastic) Gradient Descent over NDCG@K
Previous example:   linear combination of α and ω

▪BM25 can be extended to handle structured (multi-
field) documents
▪e.g., for a scholar paper: title, abstract, summary, author

▪The extended function is named BM25F:
▪ws is a weight of field s
▪ft,s is the frequency of term t in field s (of document d)
▪ls is the length of field s (of document d)
▪bs determines the importance of ls
▪Ls is the average length of field s in the collection

text:
LtR applied to BM25F
▪Given a query q and a set of documents D={d1,d2, …}
▪We want to learn a model h that assigns scores (thus enabling 
ranking by sorting in descending order of score) to documents in D 
according to their relevance
▪Results = sort {h(d1), h(d2), …}
▪where the function h is BM25F with a proper parameter set θ
▪How to apply Gradient Descent ?
▪Unlike pointwise or pairwise approaches, the listwise approaches aim to 
directly optimize the evaluation metrics such as NDCG@k or MAP@k
▪The main difficulty in optimizing these evaluation metrics is that they are 
dependent on the rank position of documents induced by the scoring 
function, not the numerical score values output by the ranking function
▪We should compute the gradient of sort w.r.t. θ
  ….but sort is not a continuous and derivable function!
▪Therefore, we cannot directly apply gradient descent
M. Taylor, H. Zaragoza, et al.  Optimisation Methods for Ranking 
Functions with Multiple Parameters. CIKM-06
[NOTE] Methods that look at all of the results in the list of result set. If a relevant doc is at the bottom, then it's very wrong but the pairwise approach does not consider this.

approximate a “sort” of NDGC  gradients
▪MART, also known as Gradient Boosted Regression Tree 
(GBRT), is an ensemble of regression trees
▪See the library: LightGBM

text:
From BOW to NIR rankings
19
▪“Bag Of Words” (BOW) 
▪presence and amount of query term occurrences (TF) of in each document, 
along with term popularity in the collection (IDF) 
▪infer the relevance of a doc to a query exploiting partially hand-crafted 
functions such as BM25 
▪Learning to Rank (LtR)
▪Many sources for collecting relevance information about documents 
(document to query) have been identified 
▪e.g., from usage/structure of the Web 
▪Machine Learning (ML) methods have been proved effective to deal with 
this abundance of relevance signals
▪The application of ML to rank has given birth to many learning-to-rank (LtR) 
models
▪Trained ML models implementing regressors, in turn realized as Boosted 
Tree Ensembles or Neural Networks
All these models implement some sort of regressor.

▪Based of Deep NN
▪Aiming to learn abstract representations of documents and

▪A promise of deep learning is to learn rich representations of 
complex objects directly from raw data
▪Increasingly relevant in NLP is the idea that we can takes data 
and attempts to unsupervisedly (or “self-supervisedly”) learn 
properties of the elements of that data 
▪(Large) Language Models

text:
Representing words by their context
24
• Distributional semantics: A word’s meaning is given by the 
words that frequently appear close-by 
• One of the most successful ideas of modern statistical NLP! 
• When the word w appears in a text, its context is the set of 
words that appear nearby (within a fixed-size window). 
• We use the many contexts of w to build up a vector representation of w 
(word embedding)

Neural word embeddings
26
• Learned methods to obtain dense word vector representations 
as part of a neural network architecture for language modelling
• Chosen so that each dense vector of a given word is similar to other

• They are a distributed representation
• Such vectors are also called (word) embeddings or (neural) 
word representations
• We can measure word similarity as vector dot (scalar) product 
or Euclidean distance

text:
Word2vec (Skip-Gram Model)
27
• Word2vec (Mikolov et al. 2013) is a framework for learning 
embeddings 
• Idea : 
• We have a large corpus (“body”) of text: a long list of words 
• Every word in a fixed vocabulary is represented by a vector
• Note that the vocabulary used depends on the tokenizer exploited and the subsequent 
normalization  
• Go through each position t in the text, which has a center word c = wt  
and various context words o 
• Use the similarity of the word vectors for c and o to calculate the 
probability of o given c (Skip-Gram Model):   P(o | c)
• Keep adjusting the word vectors to maximize this probability 
• Limit the context of the center word c within a given window
• The Continuous Bag of Words Model (CBOW) computes the 
opposite, i.e., P(c | o)
bp can be used, or just any old tokenizer.
[NOTE] bp can be used, or just any old tokenizer.

• Gradient Descent
Gradient-based methods can find “good” U = {uo} and V = {vc} for 
minimizing the loss function by starting  with a relatively uninformed guess, 
and  iteratively moving in the direction that locally best-improves the guess

▪The Corpus may have huge numbers of tokens and windows

using Full gradient!

▪Instead: We update parameters after each window t of the corpus

• Mikolov et al. introduced a new evaluation scheme based on 
word analogies
• Not only the scalar distance between word vectors
• For example, the analogy “king is to queen as man is to woman” 
• it should be encoded in the vector space by the vector equation

• This evaluation scheme favors models that produce dimensions 
of meaning
• It captures the multi-clustering idea of distributed representations

text:
Neural IR & Word embeddings
45
▪Classic IR and the “vocabulary mismatch problem”
▪query terms not matching words used in documents that are 
relevant to user's intent
▪Issues: Synonymy (different words for the same concept), 
Polysemy (same word with different meanings), Language 
variation (e.g., regional differences, jargon)
▪How to tackle this challenge in classic IR? 
1. enrich query representations to better match document 
representations (query expansion)
2. enrich document representations to better match query 
representations (enrich documents with synonymous terms)  
▪
Neural IR and Deep learning get rid of exact matching, 
beginning to use word embeddings and beyond

text:
Static vs. Contextualized Word embeddings 
2
▪Static word embeddings (e.g., Word2vec)
▪Global representations of the words, i.e., a single fixed 
embedding for each term in the vocabulary
▪Static embeddings fail with multi-sense terms (which are 
however rare) and end up with a “sense averaged” or most 
common-sense representation
▪Contextualized word embeddings 
▪Local representations of words, depending on the others used 
together within a “given instance”
▪We’ll see that transformer deep leaning architectures can 
compute contextualized word embeddings
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lectureLtR-neural_IR_2-2.pdf

text:
Static vs. Contextualized Word embeddings 
3
▪Static word embeddings (e.g., Word2vec)
▪Global representations of the words, i.e., a single fixed 
embedding for each term in the vocabulary
▪Static embeddings fail with multi-sense terms (which are 
however rare) and end up with a “sense averaged” or most 
common-sense representation
▪Contextualized word embeddings 
▪Local representations of words, depending on the others used 
together on a given instance
▪We’ll see that transformer deep leaning architectures can 
compute contextualized word embeddings
Indeed, the vocabulary terms in real application 
of Neural IR and Neural NLP are smaller in size, 
and breaks words in sub-pieces:
WordPiece Tokenizer for BERT 
Byte pair encoding (BPE) for LLMs and some 
Neural IR systems
However, in GPT or BERT (transformer deep 
leaning) the token embeddings are pre-trained 
end-to-end as part of the whole model

text:
DNNs to Contextualize Word embeddings 
4
▪Starting from static representations of words (even word 
pieces), Deep NNs (DNNs) aggregate information from 
surrounding words to contextualize the meaning of 
specific sentences 
▪For example, deciding on the most likely meaning and 
appropriate representation of the word “bank” in the sentence:
           “I arrived at the bank after crossing the…” 
requires knowing if the sentence ends in “... road.” or “... river.”
▪bank may refer to either the shore of a river or to a financial 
institution

5
▪Before Transformers
▪Recurrent neural networks (RNNs)
▪used for language understanding tasks, such as language 
modeling and machine translation (along with LSTMs and 
GRUs)
▪RNNs realize seq2seq networks
▪given an input sequence of terms, produce an output 
sequence of “translated” terms (sequentially, left to right)
▪ISSUE: strict sequential left-to-right behavior in input and 
output at both learning and inference time (see the previous 
example of “bank” term)

text:
Transformers
6
▪In “Attention Is All You Need”, the Google team 
introduced the Transformer deep NN architecture
▪Novel NN architecture based on a self-attention mechanism, 
particularly well suited for language understanding (much 
better than RNNs)
▪As other RNN methods for language translation/understanding, 
they are based on Encoders & Decoders
▪Still these DNNs transform an input sequence into an output 
sequence, but focusing differently on different parts of the 
input due to self-attentions 
B. Vaswani, et al. Attention is all you need. NIPS 2017
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lectureLtR-neural_IR_2-2.pdf

text:
Transformers
7
▪The Transformer performs a small, constant number of 
steps
▪at each step, it applies a self-attention mechanism, which 
directly models relationships between all words in a sentence, 
regardless of their respective position
▪Consider the earlier example “I arrived at the bank after 
crossing the river”
▪the Transformer can learn to pay immediately attention to the word 
“river” (at the end of the phrase), thus making the decision in a single 
step. 
▪No more left-to-right behavior
B. Vaswani, et al. Attention is all you need. NIPS 2017

text:
Transformers
▪The main novelty of transformers are:
▪self-attention layer – a layer that permits the encoder 
look at other words in the input sentence as it encodes 
a specific word. 
▪It solves the problem of  RNN networks
▪RNNs process the input sequence sequentially, one word at a 
time
▪the computation for time-step t cannot be carried out until the 
computation for time-step  t-1 have been completed 
▪In addition, RNN & friends must maintain 
in a hidden state all long-range dependencies 
between words spread far apart in a long sentence 
▪This also implies slows down training and inference.
9
B. Vaswani, et al. Attention is all you need. NIPS 2017

text:
How does Self-attention work
▪Given the current word encodings/embeddings, first create 
three vectors from each of the encoder’s input vectors
▪Query vector, Key vector, and Value vector 
▪…  by multiplying the embedding by three matrices (to be trained)
▪In the paper, their dimensionality is 64, while the dimensionality of 
embedding and encoder input/output vectors is 512

text:
How does Self-attention work
▪Second, calculate a score (we’re calculating the self-attention 
for the first word “Thinking” in this example), and  normalize 
the computed scores score, using softmax
▪We have to compute the same for the other word/query “Machines”
▪The softmax scores show the usefulness of attending the 
other words (keys) relevant to “Thinking” (query)

text:
How does Self-attention work
▪Third, multiply each value vector vi  by the associated softmax 
score, and then sum up them (weighted sum) to obtain vector zi
▪The intuition here is 
to disempower 
irrelevant words 
(by multiplying 
them by tiny numbers)
▪Finally, sum them up, and
send the zi vector to a
feed-forward layer
to produce the output 
of the encoders

text:
How does Self-attention work
17
• The decoder is much more complex
• It uses the output of the top encoder transformed into 
a set of attention vectors 
• These vectors are exploited by each decoder in its “encoder-
decoder attention” layer 
• This helps the decoder to also focus on appropriate 
places in the input sequence
• The decoder works sequentially
• At each step, it guess a term/token of the output sequence
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lectureLtR-neural_IR_2-2.pdf

text:
How does Self-attention work
▪Linear layer
▪Fully connected neural network
▪Projects the vector produced by the stack of decoders, into a much larger 
vector called a logits vector (of dimension |V|, the term vocabulary)
▪Softmax layer 
▪Turns the logits scores into probabilities
▪The cell with the highest probability is chosen, and the word associated 
with it is produced as the output for this time step
▪Vaswani et al. start with random initialization of model 
weights
▪Proceed to directly train on labeled data in a supervised manner
▪i.e., using (input sequence, output sequence) pairs
19

text:
Pre-training BERT  
25
▪“Masked Language Model” (MLM) pretraining objective
▪Randomly “cover up” (more formally, “mask”) a token from the input 
sequence
▪Ask the model to “guess” (i.e., predict) it, training with cross entropy loss
▪a.k.a. cloze task
▪B = bidirectional 
▪The MLM objective well explains the “B” in BERT, as the model can use both 
left and right contexts (preceding and succeeding contexts) of a masked 
token to make predictions
▪In contrast, as GPT is a decoder-only transformer, it uses a plain language 
modeling objective for pre-training, thus only exploiting the preceding 
tokens in the left context only (formally, this is called “autoregressive” 
model according to time-series analysis theory):
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lectureLtR-neural_IR_2-2.pdf

text:
Pre-training BERT  
26
▪Next sentence prediction (NSP) is the other training goal (besides 
MLM)
▪Pairs of sentences as input 
▪Learn to predict if the second sentence in the pair is the subsequent 
sentence in the original document 
▪During training, 50% of the inputs are a pair in which the second sentence is

text:
Tokenizers
28
▪Input sequences to BERT are usually tokenized with the WordPiece 
tokenizer [Wu et al., 2016], while BPE [Sennrich et al., 2016] is a 
common alternative (used in GPT)
▪The aim of these tokenizers is to reduce the vocabulary space by 
splitting words into “subwords”, usually in an unsupervised 
manner
▪The embeddings of BERT token are generated/learned  end-to-
end during pre-training as part of the whole model (they are not 
word2vec)
Y. Wu, et al. Google’s neural machine translation system: Bridging the gap between human and 
machine translation. arXiv:1609.08144, 2016.
R. Sennrich, et al. Neural machine translation of rare words with subword units. In ACL, 2016.

text:
Tokenizers
29
▪WordPiece is the tokenizer used by BERT
▪“scrolling” becomes “scroll” + “##ing”
▪note the convention of prepending two hashes (##) to a subword, indicating that it is 
connected to the previous subword  (no spaces between the current subword and the 
previous one)
▪ “walking” and “talking” are not split into subwords
▪“biking” is split into “bi” + “##king”
▪“biostatistics” is split into “bio” + “##sta” + “##tist” + “##ics” 
▪Finally, we obtain a small vocabulary (e.g., 30,522 wordpieces) sufficient to 
model large, naturally-occurring corpora that may have millions of unique 
tokens if based on a simple method like tokenization by whitespace
▪RoBERTa is a BERT-based DNN that uses BPE tokenizer
Y. Wu, et al. Google’s neural machine translation system: Bridging the gap between human and 
machine translation. arXiv:1609.08144, 2016.

for “re-ranking”, 2nd stage

▪BERT can also be used for a representation-based model

▪Not only re-ranking among a query-dependent sample
▪This introduces a new type of retrieval systems called dense retrieval, in 
contrast to the classic BOW sparse retrieval model
▪A representation-based model is also called dual encoder or bi-

▪Several incorrect (negative) documents d-
▪Encodes q, d+, and d- into vectors
▪Then uses dot product or cosine similarity to score them:
▪Minimize a contrastive loss like this for each query q:

text:
Dealing with long documents
▪BERT have an input size limited to 512 tokens, including the special 
ones. 
▪Long documents cannot be fed completely into a transformer model
▪We need to split them into smaller texts, in a procedure referred to as 
passaging
▪Proposal by Dai and Callan [2019]
▪Split a long document into overlapping shorter passages, processed 
independently together with the same query by a cross-encoder. 
▪During training, if a long document is relevant, all its passages are relevant, 
and vice-versa
▪At processing time, the relevance scores for each composing passage are 
aggregated back to a single score for a long document
▪Possible aggregations: FirstP, MaxP, and SumP (where P stands for Passage)
Z. Dai and J. Callan. Deeper Text Understanding for IR with Contextual Neural Language 
Modeling. In Proc. SIGIR 2019.

text:
Graph-based A-kNN 
▪
Each graph node corresponds to a distinct document vector, while 
each edge stores the distance/similarity between vectors
▪
Computing exact kNN with a graph requires to compute and store 
O(n2) similarity!    UNFEASIBLE 
▪
Graph-based A-kNN navigates an incomplete graph 
▪Starting from a predefined entry node, the graph is visited one node at a 
time, keeping on finding the closest nodes to query vectors among the 
unvisited neighbour nodes. 
▪The search terminates when there is no improvement in the current kNN 
candidate set (minHeap)
▪This simple technique is however inefficient due to long paths potentially 
required to navigate the graph to identify the nodes/documents closest 
to the query 
51

text:
HNSW & Greedy Search
▪Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graph 
▪The HNSW index stores the input data into multiple NSW graphs
▪The search procedure starts with the top layer graph. 
▪At each layer, the greedy heuristic searches for the closest node, then the next layer 
is searched, starting from the node corresponding to the closest node identified in 
the preceding graph
▪At the bottom layer, the greedy heuristic searches for the k closest nodes to be 
returned starting from the node identified navigating the previous layers 
The bottom layer 
contains a node for each 
document/element
The quantity of 
nodes in the other 
graphs decreases 
exponentially at 
each layer

text:
Static vs. Contextualized Word embeddings 
2
▪Static word embeddings (e.g., Word2vec)
▪Global representations of the words, i.e., a single fixed 
embedding for each term in the vocabulary
▪Static embeddings fail with multi-sense terms (which are 
however rare) and end up with a “sense averaged” or most 
common-sense representation
▪Contextualized word embeddings 
▪Local representations of words, depending on the others used 
together within a “given instance”
▪We’ll see that transformer deep leaning architectures can 
compute contextualized word embeddings

▪Static word embeddings (e.g., Word2vec)
▪Global representations of the words, i.e., a single fixed 
embedding for each term in the vocabulary
▪Static embeddings fail with multi-sense terms (which are 
however rare) and end up with a “sense averaged” or most 
common-sense representation
▪Contextualized word embeddings 
▪Local representations of words, depending on the others used 
together on a given instance
▪We’ll see that transformer deep leaning architectures can 
compute contextualized word embeddings
Indeed, the vocabulary terms in real application 
of Neural IR and Neural NLP are smaller in size, 
and breaks words in sub-pieces:
WordPiece Tokenizer for BERT

Neural IR systems

text:
DNNs to Contextualize Word embeddings 
4
▪Starting from static representations of words (even word 
pieces), Deep NNs (DNNs) aggregate information from 
surrounding words to contextualize the meaning of 
specific sentences 
▪For example, deciding on the most likely meaning and 
appropriate representation of the word “bank” in the sentence:
           “I arrived at the bank after crossing the…” 
requires knowing if the sentence ends in “... road.” or “... river.”
▪bank may refer to either the shore of a river or to a financial 
institution
banka / riva ?

text:
DNNs to Contextualize Word embeddings 
5
▪Before Transformers
▪Recurrent neural networks (RNNs)
▪used for language understanding tasks, such as language 
modeling and machine translation (along with LSTMs and 
GRUs)
▪RNNs realize seq2seq networks
▪given an input sequence of terms, produce an output 
sequence of “translated” terms (sequentially, left to right)
▪ISSUE: strict sequential left-to-right behavior in input and 
output at both learning and inference time (see the previous 
example of “bank” term)

Transformers
6
▪In “Attention Is All You Need”, the Google team 
introduced the Transformer deep NN architecture
▪Novel NN architecture based on a self-attention mechanism, 
particularly well suited for language understanding (much 
better than RNNs)
▪As other RNN methods for language translation/understanding, 
they are based on Encoders & Decoders
▪Still these DNNs transform an input sequence into an output 
sequence, but focusing differently on different parts of the 
input due to self-attentions

text:
Transformers
▪The main novelty of transformers are:
▪self-attention layer – a layer that permits the encoder 
look at other words in the input sentence as it encodes 
a specific word. 
▪It solves the problem of  RNN networks
▪RNNs process the input sequence sequentially, one word at a 
time
▪the computation for time-step t cannot be carried out until the 
computation for time-step  t-1 have been completed 
▪In addition, RNN & friends must maintain 
in a hidden state all long-range dependencies 
between words spread far apart in a long sentence 
▪This also implies slows down training and inference.

text:
How does Self-attention work
17
• The decoder is much more complex
• It uses the output of the top encoder transformed into 
a set of attention vectors 
• These vectors are exploited by each decoder in its “encoder-
decoder attention” layer 
• This helps the decoder to also focus on appropriate 
places in the input sequence
• The decoder works sequentially
• At each step, it guess a term/token of the output sequence

text:
How does Self-attention work
▪Linear layer
▪Fully connected neural network
▪Projects the vector produced by the stack of decoders, into a much larger 
vector called a logits vector (of dimension |V|, the term vocabulary)
▪Softmax layer 
▪Turns the logits scores into probabilities
▪The cell with the highest probability is chosen, and the word associated 
with it is produced as the output for this time step
▪Vaswani et al. start with random initialization of model 
weights
▪Proceed to directly train on labeled data in a supervised manner
▪i.e., using (input sequence, output sequence) pairs

text:
Pre-training BERT  
25
▪“Masked Language Model” (MLM) pretraining objective
▪Randomly “cover up” (more formally, “mask”) a token from the input 
sequence
▪Ask the model to “guess” (i.e., predict) it, training with cross entropy loss
▪a.k.a. cloze task
▪B = bidirectional 
▪The MLM objective well explains the “B” in BERT, as the model can use both 
left and right contexts (preceding and succeeding contexts) of a masked 
token to make predictions
▪In contrast, GPT uses a language modeling objective for pre-training, thus 
only exploiting the preceding tokens in the left context only (formally, this is 
called “autoregressive” model)

text:
Tokenizers
29
▪WordPiece is the tokenizer used by BERT
▪“scrolling” becomes “scroll” + “##ing”
▪note the convention of prepending two hashes (##) to a subword, indicating that it is 
connected to the previous subword  (no spaces between the current subword and the 
previous one)
▪ “walking” and “talking” are not split into subwords
▪“biking” is split into “bi” + “##king”
▪“biostatistics” is split into “bio” + “##sta” + “##tist” + “##ics” 
▪Finally, we obtain a small vocabulary (e.g., 30,522 wordpieces) sufficient to 
model large, naturally-occurring corpora that may have millions of unique 
tokens if based on a simple method like tokenization by whitespace
▪RoBERTa is a BERT-based DNN that uses Byte-level BPE tokenizer
Y. Wu, et al. Google’s neural machine translation system: Bridging the gap between human and 
machine translation. arXiv:1609.08144, 2016.
R. Sennrich, et al. Neural machine translation of rare words with subword units. In ACL, 2016.

text:
Dealing with long documents
▪BERT have an input size limited to 512 tokens, including the special 
ones. 
▪Long documents cannot be fed completely into a transformer model
▪We need to split them into smaller texts, in a procedure referred to as 
passaging
▪Proposal by Dai and Callan [2019]
▪Split a long document into overlapping shorter passages, processed 
independently together with the same query by a cross-encoder. 
▪During training, if a long document is relevant, all its passages are relevant, 
and vice-versa
▪At processing time, the relevance scores for each composing passage are 
aggregated back to a single score for a long document
▪Possible aggregations: FirstP, MaxP, and SumP (where P stands for Passage)
Z. Dai and J. Callan. Deeper Text Understanding for IR with Contextual Neural Language 
Modeling. In Proc. SIGIR 2019.
source_path: D:\Ca' Foscari\Semester 2\Information rtrival and web search\lectureLtR-neural_IR_2.pdf

text:
Fine-tuning BERT as a bi-encoder
for “dense retrieval”, used as first stage retrieval
▪BERT can also be used for a representation-based model 
▪Goal: build up independent query and document representations
▪Document representations can be pre-computed and stored in advance
▪At query processing time, only the query representation is computed
▪The top documents are searched through the stored document 
representations 
▪Representation-based systems can identify the relevant 
documents among all documents in a collection
▪Not only re-ranking among a query-dependent sample
▪This introduces a new type of retrieval systems called dense retrieval, in 
contrast to the classic BOW sparse retrieval model
▪A representation-based model is also called dual encoder or bi-
encoder

text:
Fine-tuning BERT as a bi-encoder
for “dense retrieval”, used as first stage retrieval
▪For fine-tuning BERT used as bi-encoder, use Contrastive Loss
▪Pull together matching query-document pairs (positive pairs)
▪Push apart non-matching ones (negative pairs)
▪In the training batch you have:
▪A query q 
▪Its correct (positive) document d+
▪Several incorrect (negative) documents d-
▪Encodes q, d+, and d- into vectors
▪Then uses dot product or cosine similarity to score them:
▪Minimize a contrastive loss like this:
that pushes up the similarity for the positive pair and pushes down the 
scores of negative pairs.

▪
The goal of the maximum inner product (MIP) search is to find the document 
embedding such that the inner product (similarity) is maximum
▪
The naive Document embedding index is the flat index, that requires an exhaustive 
search to the most similar document
▪
The most recent search methods have shifted to Approximate - k Nearest Neighbor 
(A-kNN) search, e.g., using the k-means clustering algorithm to partition the index
▪
In the IR research community, Meta’s FAISS is the most widely adopted framework

text:
Graph-based A-kNN 
▪
Each graph node corresponds to a distinct document vector, while 
each edge stores the distance/similarity between vectors
▪
Computing exact kNN with a graph requires to compute and store 
O(n2) similarity!    UNFEASIBLE 
▪
Graph-based A-kNN navigates an incomplete graph 
▪Starting from a predefined entry node, the graph is visited one node at a 
time, keeping on finding the closest nodes to query vectors among the 
unvisited neighbour nodes. 
▪The search terminates when there is no improvement in the current kNN 
candidate set (minHeap)
▪This simple technique is however inefficient due to long paths potentially 
required to navigate the graph to identify the nodes/documents closest 
to the query 
50

Navigable small world

only short-range 
edges, i.e., edges

close nodes, the 
kNN graph can be 
enriched with 
randomly 
generated long-
range edges
▪Long-range edges aim to transform the incomplete graph in a small word one, 
with short average path length
▪any two nodes are connected by surprisingly few hops (small degree of separation)
▪Example:
▪starting from an entry node, navigate the graph to return the 1NN result set

text:
HNSW & Greedy Search
▪Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graph 
▪The HNSW index stores the input data into multiple NSW graphs
▪The search procedure starts with the top layer graph. 
▪At each layer, the greedy heuristic searches for the closest node, then the next layer 
is searched, starting from the node corresponding to the closest node identified in 
the preceding graph
▪At the bottom layer, the greedy heuristic searches for the k closest nodes to be 
returned starting from the node identified navigating the previous layers 
The bottom layer 
contains a node for each 
document/element
The quantity of 
nodes in the other 
graphs decreases 
exponentially at 
each layer

text:
Learned Sparse Retrieval
53
▪
Novel Neural IR – based proposal
▪
Incorporate the effectiveness improvements of neural networks into inverted indexes (with their 
efficient query processing algorithms) through learned sparse retrieval approaches
▪
Still a representation-based (sparse) approach, based on a small lexicon (Wordpiece or BPE)  
▪
Document/query expansion learning
▪seq2seq neural models to modify the actual content of documents/queries
▪
Impact score learning
▪the output embeddings of documents’ terms are furtherly transformed with neural 
networks to generate a single real value  (relevance contribution of the term in the 
document)
▪
Sparse representation learning 
▪instead of independently learning to expand the documents/queries and then 
learning the impact score of the terms in the expanded documents, sparse 
representation learning aims at learning both at the same time
▪Notable example: SPLADE ver. 1, 2, etc.

text:
DocID reassignment - TSP
▪A technique proposed in the literature for DocID reassignment is 
based on TSP
▪The heuristic computes a pairwise distance between every pair of 
documents ( complete distance matrix!! )
▪Distance proportional to the number of shared terms (documents 
as sets):
      e.g., Jaccard distance = 1 – JaccardSim
▪ 1: max distance       0: identical documents
▪Two documents di and dj have a high value of JaccardSim (small 
distance) if they share many terms
▪Limit case:  
▪Assign two consecutive DocIDs to di and dj
▪Let Tshd be the set of shared terms between di and dj
▪For each t ∈ Tshd the gaps in the associated posting lists between the two docs 
di and dj will be equal to 1, i.e., max compression

text:
What is clustering?
▪Clustering: the process of grouping a set of objects 
into classes of similar objects
▪Documents within a cluster should be similar.
▪Documents from different clusters should be 
dissimilar.
▪The commonest form of unsupervised learning
▪Unsupervised learning = learning from raw data, as 
opposed to supervised data where a classification of 
examples is given
▪A common and important task that finds many 
applications in IR and other places

DocID reassignment:

▪How to cluster million of items/documents
▪Single scan clustering reduces computational costs
▪scan linearly the documents
▪each cluster returned will be identified by a medoid, i.e., a 
document that represents all the others in the cluster
▪the medoid should be the most centrally located point in 
the cluster. However, the stream nature of the clustering 
algorithm does not guarantee this property of medoids
▪Heuristics to reduce Jaccard distance
▪sort documents in reverse order of length 
▪a doc with many terms should be closer to much more docs

text:
DocID reassignment:
 
possible scalable solution (single scan k-means)
▪
The pseudo-code of the stream algorithm that visits each document only 
once is the following, where Radius is the hyperparameter. 
▪
Another hyperparameter should be the maximum number of clusters, 
allowing the remaining documents to be assigned similarly to k-means.
Stream_cluster(SortedCollection, Radius)
     C = ∅
     for each d in SortedCollection
         Dist_c = Min (JaccardDistance(c, d), for each medoid c in C)
         if (Dist_c < Radius) then
             add d to cluster c
         else
             make d a new medoid, and add it to C:   C = C ∪ d
     return C
